entries.title: Buddy Guy joins star-studded ensemble for live performance of ‘I Lied to You’ from ‘Sinners’ at the Oscars
entries.summary: <p>Buddy Guy took to Hollywood’s biggest stage Sunday night with a star-studded lineup to bring a pivotal scene about the power of music in “Sinners” to life.</p><p>Guy wore a black and white polka dot shirt under black leather overalls as he played the guitar alongside Miles Caton and Raphael Saadiq, who wrote the song with composer Ludwig Göransson. Misty Copeland, Eric Gales, Brittany Howard, Christone “Kingfish” Ingram, Jayme Lawson, Li Jun Li, Bobby Rush, Shaboozey and Alice Smith were also part of the performance, which faithfully recreated the scene at the juke joint when Sammie Moore, portrayed by Caton, conjures up the spirits of musicians from past and present, including African tribal dancers, a DJ and break dancers, while singing and playing the guitar during “I Lied To You,” a song about his love of the blues.</p><p>Guy has a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/music/2026/02/25/buddy-guy-sinners-movie-ryan-coogler-blues-music" target="_blank" >brief but significant cameo</a> as the older Sammie in the movie. The legendary Chicago blues musician’s impact on the genre is one of the reasons “Sinners” writer and director Ryan Coogler wanted Guy in his film, and the writer and director personally visited Buddy Guy’s Legends to ask him to be a part of it.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-020000" name="image-020000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" Jordan, Wunmi Mosaku and Delroy Lindo as well as best picture, best original screenplay and best original score.</p><p>The producers behind the 98th Academy Awards set out to celebrate not just the song, but the cultural phenomenon of the film itself. “Golden” from “KPop Demon Hunters,” which won best animated film, was the other nominee for best original song scheduled to be performed live.</p><p>“We have two really unique stories that are going to be celebrated on the stage,” said Oscars executive producer and showrunner Raj Kapoor said ahead of the broadcast. “They’re bigger than just the songs. They’re really about filmmaking and craft.”</p><p><i>Contributing: AP</i></p>
entries.author: Dorothy Hernandez

entries.title: South Side Irish Parade brings Irish pride, old and new, for Chicagoans
entries.summary: <p>On yet another rainy day in March, 47 years after Morgan Park residents George Hendry and Pat Coakley started their own South Side Irish Parade, a crowd was once again waiting for the bagpipers and Irish dancers to march by.</p><p>As it has since 1981, the parade stepped off from 103rd Street down Western Avenue to 115th Street — despite intermittent sheets of rain and gusts of wind.</p><p>Tom Wognum, who brought his 8-year-old son Milo for the eighth time, said nothing would deter the duo.</p><p>“We have been out here in the snow, we have been out here in shorts and T-shirts,” Wognum said. “It’s Chicago in March, there’s no way to predict any of it. But whatever the weather, we’re going to come out.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Members of the Shannon Rovers march in the South Side Irish Parade in Beverly Sunday March 15, 2026. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Violet Miller/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The original 79th Street South Side Irish parade was moved downtown in 1960. In 1979, Hendry and Coakley started their own parade of local folks down Washtenaw and Talman avenues at 109th Street in their Morgan Park neighbohood. Two years later, they moved it to its current home along Western.</p><p>Wognum comes for the Irish bands — a family friend used to play bagpipes in the CPD band and another family friend once played bass drum in the Shannon Rovers — and his son comes for the firetrucks, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/business/2021/7/22/22588900/scabby-the-rat-survives-national-labor-relations-board-nlrb-inflatables-union-protests" target="_blank" ><u>Scabby the Rat</u></a> and as many 3 Musketeers bars as he can find.</p><p>But their connection to the parade is deeper than that. Wognum grew up five blocks away, first attending in 1980, and can point to his current home from where the two stood catching freebies.</p><p>Wognum isn’t Irish but said he cherishes childhood memories of marching alongside his father in the Boy Scouts. He enjoys himself even more with his son alongside him, especially now he no longer has to walk the full route.</p><p>“I enjoy the atmosphere, everything about it has always been fun,” Wognum said. “I'm just happy it’s still going on and I get to pass this down.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>DePaul students Grace Murphy, Cecilia Pelanza and Ava Patrowski cheer at the South Side Irish Parade Sunday March 15, 2026. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Violet Miller/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>DePaul students Grace Murphy, Cecilia Pelanza and Ava Patrowski came to the parade with a group of friends after spending Saturday downtown <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago/2026/03/14/a-rite-of-passage-in-emerald-green" target="_blank" >watching the Chicago River being dyed green</a>. The parade, they said, was a breath of fresh air.</p><p>“This feels so much more community and family-centered,” said Pelanza, 18. “The river is very cool, but you barely see it, and you’re in a giant crowd. Here, you’re actually interacting with everybody.”</p><p>For Murphy, who grew up in Mount Greenwood, and Patrowski — who watched the parade from her family’s doorstep growing up — it was standard fare. But Massachusetts-raised Pelanza found new pride in her Irish background.</p><p>Her family hadn’t been big on the holiday, she said, and there was nothing like this at home. But she’s already planning to come back next year seeking the same community feeling.</p><p>“I didn’t have this growing up,” Pelanza said. “Chicago has turned me into a big St. Paddy’s fan, a big Irish pride individual. I love being Irish.”</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li entries.author: Violet Miller

entries.title: 'One Battle After Another' wins best picture at 98th Academy Awards
entries.summary: <p>LOS ANGELES — Paul Thomas Anderson’s “One Battle After Another” was crowned best picture at the 98th Academy Awards, handing Hollywood’s top honor to a comic, multi-generational American saga of political resistance.</p><p>The ceremony Sunday, which also saw Michael B. Jordan win best actor and “Sinners” cinematographer Autumn Durald Arkapaw make Oscar history as the first female director of photography to win the award, was a long-in-coming coronation for Anderson, a San Fernando Valley native who made his first short at age 18 and has been one of America’s most lionized filmmakers for decades. Before Sunday, Anderson had never won an Oscar.</p><p>But “One Battle After Another,” the favorite coming in, won six Oscars, including best director and best adapted screenplay for Anderson, the Oscars' first trophy for best casting and best supporting actor for an absent Sean Penn.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-a20000" name="image-a20000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/0e2162a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+54/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fac%2F83%2Fc7753922423e84b820487315e678%2Fap26075088440617.jpg class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Paul Thomas Anderson accepts the award for directing for “One Battle After Another” during the Oscars on Sunday at the Dolby Theatre in Los Angeles.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Chris Pizzello/Invision/AP</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>“I wrote this movie for my kids to say sorry for the housekeeping mess that we left in this world — we’re handing off to them,” said Anderson while accepting the screenplay trophy. “But also with the encouragement that they will be the generation that hopefully brings us some common sense and decency.”</p><p>“Sinners,” which came in with a record 16 nominations, also landed some big and even historic wins. Coogler, the widely loved filmmaker, won the first Oscar in an unblemished career that started out with Jordan in 2013's “Fruitvale Station.” Arkapow, only the fourth female cinematographer ever nominated, won the award in a long-in-coming triumph for women behind the camera.</p><p>“I really want all the women in room to stand up,” said Arkapaw. “Because I don’t feel like I get here without you guys.”</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a ‘Sinners’ at the Oscars</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/movies-and-tv/2026/03/07/richard-roepers-2026-oscars-predictions-are-here" >Richard Roeper’s 2026 Oscars predictions are here</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/32bb6bf/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+54/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fdf%2F4e%2F248eb12f4256880dc5d49bd8776c%2Fap26075069137851.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/24b0f82/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+54/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fdf%2F4e%2F248eb12f4256880dc5d49bd8776c%2Fap26075069137851.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Autumn Durald Arkapaw accepts the award for cinematography for "Sinners" during the Oscars on Sunday, March 15, 2026, at the Dolby Theatre in Los Angeles. (AP Photo/Chris Pizzello)" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/24b0f82/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+54/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fdf%2F4e%2F248eb12f4256880dc5d49bd8776c%2Fap26075069137851.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7fe47c7/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+54/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fdf%2F4e%2F248eb12f4256880dc5d49bd8776c%2Fap26075069137851.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/24b0f82/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+54/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fdf%2F4e%2F248eb12f4256880dc5d49bd8776c%2Fap26075069137851.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Autumn Durald Arkapaw accepts the award for cinematography for “Sinners” during the Oscars on Sunday at the Dolby Theatre in Los Angeles. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Chris Pizzello/Chris Pizzello/Invision/AP</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>And Jordan, one of Hollywood's most liked leading men, won best actor in one of the night's closest races. The Dolby Theatre rose to its feet in the most thunderous applause of the night.</p><p>“Yo, momma, what’s up?” said Jordan after staggering to the stage.</p><p>The Oscar night belonged to Warner Bros., the studio of “One Battle After Another” and Coogler’s vampire tale. It was an oddly poignant note of triumph for the fabled studio, which weeks earlier agreed to a sale to Paramount Skydance, David Ellison’s rapidly assembled media monolith. The deal, which awaits regulatory approval, has Hollywood bracing for more layoffs.</p><p>“Sinners” and “One Battle After Another” were each Hollywood anomalies: big-budget originals born from a personal vision. In a year where anxiety over studio contraction and the rise of artificial intelligence often consumed the industry, both films gave Hollywood fresh hope.</p><p>Jessie Buckley won best actress for her performance as Agnes Shakespeare in “Hamnet,” making her the first Irish performer to ever win in the category. At an Oscars where no other acting award seemed a sure thing, Buckley cruised into Sunday’s Oscars at the Dolby Theatre as the overwhelming favorite.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-7f0000" name="image-7f0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f4dd46d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+54/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F84%2Ffd%2F7d6de675445395eab5d98d6fc386%2Fap26075095532339.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9170187/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+54/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F84%2Ffd%2F7d6de675445395eab5d98d6fc386%2Fap26075095532339.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d1dc36e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+54/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F84%2Ffd%2F7d6de675445395eab5d98d6fc386%2Fap26075095532339.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Jessie Buckley accepts the award for actress in a leading role for "Hamnet" during the Oscars on Sunday, March 15, 2026, at the Dolby Theatre in Los Angeles. (AP Photo/Chris Pizzello)" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d1dc36e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+54/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F84%2Ffd%2F7d6de675445395eab5d98d6fc386%2Fap26075095532339.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d78ed29/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+54/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F84%2Ffd%2F7d6de675445395eab5d98d6fc386%2Fap26075095532339.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d1dc36e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+54/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F84%2Ffd%2F7d6de675445395eab5d98d6fc386%2Fap26075095532339.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Jessie Buckley accepts the award for actress in a leading role for “Hamnet” during the Oscars on Sunday at the Dolby Theatre in Los Angeles. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Chris Pizzello/Invision/AP</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>"It's Mother's Day in the U.K.," said Buckley on the stage. "I would like to dedicated this to the beautiful chaos of a mother's heart."</p><h3><b>‘KPop’ and ‘Frankeinstein’ win for Netflix</b></h3><p>From the start, when host Conan O'Brien sprinted through the year's nominees as Amy Madigan's character in the horror thriller “Weapons” in a pre-taped bit, Sunday's ceremony was quirky, a little clunky and preoccupied with the shifting place of movies in culture. There was, of all things, a tie for best live-action short film.</p><p>As expected, the Netflix sensation “KPop Demon Hunters,” 2025’s most-watched film, won best animated feature, as well as best song for “Golden." It was a big win for Netflix but a more qualified victory for the movie’s producer, Sony Pictures. Though it developed and produced the film, Sony sold “KPop Demon Hunters” to the streaming giant instead of giving it a theatrical release.</p><p>On Netflix, “KPop Demon Hunters” became a cultural phenomenon and the streaming platform’s biggest hit. It has more than 325 million views and counting.</p><p>“This is for Korea and Koreans everywhere,” said co-director Maggie Kang.</p><p>Another Netflix release, Guillermo del Toro’s “Frankenstein” picked up three awards for its lavish craft, for costume design, makeup and hairstyling and for production design.</p><p>Amy Madigan won best supporting actress for her performance in the horror thriller “Weapons,” a win that came 40 years after the 75-year-old actor was first nominated, in 1986, for “Twice in a Lifetime.” Letting out a giant laugh as she hit the stage, Madigan exclaimed, “This is great!”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-0e0000" name="image-0e0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/887ead6/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+54/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F31%2Fac%2F227ccbe44f81b4de43e254707f7a%2Fap26074844770562.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ad516c2/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+54/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F31%2Fac%2F227ccbe44f81b4de43e254707f7a%2Fap26074844770562.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Amy Madigan accepts the award for actress in a supporting role for "Weapons" during the Oscars on Sunday, March 15, 2026, at the Dolby Theatre in Los Angeles. (AP Photo/Chris Pizzello)" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ad516c2/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+54/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F31%2Fac%2F227ccbe44f81b4de43e254707f7a%2Fap26074844770562.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/caa4a61/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+54/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F31%2Fac%2F227ccbe44f81b4de43e254707f7a%2Fap26074844770562.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ad516c2/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+54/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F31%2Fac%2F227ccbe44f81b4de43e254707f7a%2Fap26074844770562.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Amy Madigan accepts the award for actress in a supporting role for “Weapons” during the Oscars on Sunday at the Dolby Theatre in Los Angeles.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Chris Pizzello/Invision/AP</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><b>O’Brien presides over a ceremony shadowed by politics</b></h3><p>Hosting for the second time, O'Brien began the Dolby Theatre show alluding to “chaotic and frightening times." But he argued that the current geopolitical climate made the Oscars all the more resonate as a globally unifying force.</p><p>"We pay tribute tonight, not just to film, but to the ideals of global artistry, collaboration, patience, resilience and that rarest of qualities today — optimism,” O'Brien said. “We’re going to celebrate. Not because we think all is well, but because we work, and hope, for better.”</p><p>Throughout the show, O'Brien hit a number of targets, like Timothée Chalamet for his diss of opera and ballet. But the ceremony seldom wasn't shadowed by politics, whether in references to changes under U.S. President Donald Trump or the recently launched war in Iran.</p><p>Joachim Trier, whose Norwegian family drama “Sentimental Value” won best international film, quoted James Baldwin in his acceptance speech.</p><p>“All adults are responsible for all children,” he said. “Let’s not vote for politicians that don’t take this seriously into account.”</p><p>Presenter Jimmy Kimmel, whose late-night show last year was suspended after comments he made about Charlie Kirk's killing, was among the most blunt.</p><p>“There are some countries that don’t support free speech,” said Kimmel. “I’m not at liberty to say which. Let’s just leave it at North Korea and CBS.”</p><p>Shortly after, “Mr. Nobody Against Putin,” a film about a Russian primary school teacher who documents his students' indoctrination to support Russia's war with Ukraine, won best documentary.</p><p>“'Mr. Nobody Against Putin' is about how you lose your country,” co-director said. “And what we saw when working with this footage is that you lose it through countless, small, little acts of complicity.”</p><p>“We all face a moral choice," he added, "but, luckily, a nobody is more powerful than you think."</p><h3>Tributes to Reiner, Redford and others</h3><p>Elegy also marked the Oscars. Producers expanded the in memoriam segment following a year that featured the deaths of so many Hollywood legends, including Keaton, Robert Duvall and Redford. Barbra Streisand spoke about Redford, her “The Way We Were” co-star.</p><p>“Bob had real backbone," said Streisand, who called Redford “an intellectual cowboy" before singing a few bars of “The Way We Were.”</p><p>Billy Crystal paid tribute to Rob and Michele Reiner, who were killed in their home in December. Crystal, a close friend of Rob Reiner's who memorably starred in 1989's “When Harry Met Sally...” and 1987's “Princess Bride.” In his moving remarks, Crystal quoted the latter.</p><p>“All we can say is: Buddy, how much fun we had storming the castle,” said Crystal.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-090000" name="image-090000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/19786de/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+54/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe9%2F45%2F3892e8924c36b168e8d5c265b3fe%2Fap26075085545993.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a3416e9/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+54/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe9%2F45%2F3892e8924c36b168e8d5c265b3fe%2Fap26075085545993.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/04e2a01/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+54/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe9%2F45%2F3892e8924c36b168e8d5c265b3fe%2Fap26075085545993.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Michael B. Jordan accepts the award for actor in a leading role for "Sinners" during the Oscars on Sunday, March 15, 2026, at the Dolby Theatre in Los Angeles. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/04e2a01/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+54/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe9%2F45%2F3892e8924c36b168e8d5c265b3fe%2Fap26075085545993.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/035ce55/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+54/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe9%2F45%2F3892e8924c36b168e8d5c265b3fe%2Fap26075085545993.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/04e2a01/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+54/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe9%2F45%2F3892e8924c36b168e8d5c265b3fe%2Fap26075085545993.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Michael B. Jordan accepts the award for actor in a leading role for “Sinners” during the Oscars on Sunday at the Dolby Theatre in Los Angeles.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Chris Pizzello/Chris Pizzello/Invision/AP</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3>Theatrical looks to best streaming, again</h3><p>The night’s final award again didn't go to a streaming release; Apple's “CODA” remains the only streaming film to achieve that distinction. “Sinners” and “One Battle After Another” were both theatrical releases shot on film. And both came from Warner Bros., the legacy studio that’s agreed to merge with David Ellison’s new media colossus, Paramount Skydance. The $111 billion deal, which awaits regulatory approval, has rattled an industry already reconciling itself to the acquisitions of MGM (by Amazon) and 20th Century Fox (by The Walt Disney Co.).</p><p>Apple's “F1,” a movie that it partnered with Warner Bros. to distribute theatrically, won for best sound. The lone blockbuster of the year to go home with a win was “Avatar: Fire and Ash,” for visual effects.</p><p>Some of O’Brien’s best digs came at the expense of the streamers. Netflix chief Ted Sarandos, he joked, was in a theater for the first time. The host also lamented the lack of nominees for Amazon MGM: “Why isn’t the website I order toilet paper from winning more Oscars?”</p><p>“I’m honored to be the last human host of the Academy Awards,” said O’Brien. “Next year it’s going to be a Waymo in a tux.”<br></p>
entries.author: Jake Coyle | AP

entries.title: Illinois' path to Sweet 16 is nice, but then? Trouble — real trouble
entries.summary: <p>OK, then, let’s start with why this whole <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/college-sports" target="_blank" >NCAA Tournament</a> deal could work out very nicely for Illinois, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/college-sports/2026/03/15/illinois-penn-illini-brad-underwood-fran-mccaffery-ncaa-tournament-march-madness" target="_blank" >the No. 3 seed in the South Region</a>. In no particular order:</p><p>They can score — a lot — on any opponent. The body of work for a team that ranked No. 1 in the country in offensive efficiency (per KenPom) for much of the season is not to be taken lightly. They’d still be at the top, too, if only Purdue hadn’t shredded Michigan’s vaunted defense Sunday in a Big Ten tournament championship-game upset at the United Center. Coming in at No. 2 in efficiency by a jersey thread behind the Boilermakers is still awfully good.</p><p>They’re seventh in the country in rebounding differential. If the 24-8 Illini — the tallest team in college basketball — weren’t able to snatch it off the glass with the best of ’em, they’d have some serious explaining to do. Then again, this hasn’t been as much of a strength of late, and that has to turn back around immediately.</p><p>They have a major scorer in freshman guard Keaton Wagler, another freshman in 6-9 David Mirkovic who often appears to be their most gifted all-around player, all kinds of three-point shooting and did we mention the height thing?</p><p>They also have a nice-looking path to the Sweet 16. First up is No. 14 seed Penn on Thursday in Greenville, South Carolina. The Quakers (18-11) won the Ivy League tournament in wild fashion — with an 88-84 overtime upset of top-seeded Yale in which 6-9 forward TJ Power had 44 points, including six in the final seven seconds of regulation, and 14 boards — but they were only third in the Ivy in the regular-season standings. Next, on Saturday, could be No. 6 seed North Carolina, whose top scorer and rebounder, potential top-five NBA Caleb Wilson, broke a thumb less than two weeks ago and is out for the season.</p><p>Afraid of the third nerds from Penn? Or the broken Heels from UNC? Please.</p><p>There’s also the fact Illini coach Brad Underwood — who has predicted the Final Four for this team from the start — is <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/college-sports/2026/03/13/illinois-illini-wisconsin-badgers-big-ten-ncaa-tournament-march-madness-big-dance" target="_blank" >still sounding off with the usual confident notes</a>. You know, for what it’s worth.</p><p>“We’re really good,” he said after losing in overtime to Wisconsin in a one-and-done Big Ten tournament appearance. “We’re a possession away in a lot of games from being one of the best teams, record-wise, in the country. I still think we’re one of the best teams in the country.”</p><p>Which brings us to Houston, also one of the best teams in the country, except there’s no doubt about it when it comes to the second-seeded Cougars. It also brings us to my colleague Rob Miech, who <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/casinos-gambling/2026/03/14/ncaa-tournament-picks-illinois-florida-duke-arizona-final-four-florida-atlantic-uconn-iowa-saint-louis-villanova-virginia" target="_blank" >predicted in the Sun-Times last week</a> that Illinois will cut down the nets as national champs. I’m pretty sure Rob misspelled “Houston,” or perhaps “Arizona” or “Duke” or you get the picture. The Cougars, with their dynamite set of guards, are the blueprint of a potential Sweet 16 opponent that would give Illinois’ suspect defense fits. And nobody plays harder than coach Kelvin Sampson’s Houston teams, a compliment that hasn’t been paid to Underwood’s last couple of teams.</p><p>Champs? The Illini? Sorry, not seeing it. Final Four? Sorry again. Sweet 16? Sure, but that might be more of a ceiling than it is a base camp for an even higher climb.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/college-sports/2026/03/15/illinois-penn-illini-brad-underwood-fran-mccaffery-ncaa-tournament-march-madness" >Illinois a No. 3 seed in NCAA Tournament, will face No. 14 Penn on Thursday in South Carolina</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/college-sports/2026/03/15/my-ncaa-tournament-pick-arizona-wildcats-big-12-final-four-march-madness" >My NCAA Tournament pick: Arizona will win it all in a Big 12-heavy Final Four</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/college-sports/2026/03/13/illinois-illini-wisconsin-badgers-big-ten-ncaa-tournament-march-madness-big-dance" >With Big Dance next, is Illinois a fatally flawed team? Has to be asked after déjà vu loss to Wisconsin</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/college-sports/2026/03/14/michigan-wolverines-big-ten-best-yaxel-lendenborg-aday-mara-dusty-may-ncaa-march-madness" target="_blank" >Is Michigan the Big Ten’s best team since its last national title in 2000? Let’s get into the pecking order</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p><h2><b>Three-dot dash</b></h2><p>•<b> BUT WAIT, PENN’s</b> leading scorer is Ethan Roberts, from Arlington Heights (Hersey), who must be thrilled to get a crack at his home state’s flagship program. Even more excited might be first-year Quakers coach Fran McCaffery, who was fired by Iowa after last season, his 15th with the Illini rival. Oh, how some Illini fans must miss the ritual act of giving McCaffery grief.</p><p>“Congrats to Fran,” Underwood said. “Great, great friend.” …</p><p>•<b> “PURDUE TO A 2”</b> would’ve been a nice headline after the Boilermakers upset Michigan 80-72 for their fourth win in four days. But what I really want to know is if this suddenly red-hot team — having vaulted into the No. 2 seed in the West — has turned things all the way around after fading to seventh place in the Big Ten and become the team poll voters originally expected. In case you forgot, this was the No. 1-ranked team in the preseason Top 25. …</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">Figured you'd want some highlights of the win over the Wolverines. <a href="https://t.co/JKRTpLlXvB">https://t.co/JKRTpLlXvB</a></p>— Purdue Men's Basketball (@BoilerBall) <a href="https://twitter.com/BoilerBall/status/2033349049961627965?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">March 16, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>•<b> ANOTHER QUESTION:</b> Are we sure Midwest No. 1 seed Michigan is the Big Ten’s best shot at ending the conference’s quarter-century-long national title drought? The Wolverines have such a strong team, or else they couldn’t possibly have won a league-record 19 games in the regular season, but they clearly miss injured guard L.J. Cason, who gave them a real edge off the bench. And Purdue has point guard Braden Smith, who has never played better than he did in Chicago, setting a tournament record with 46 assists. For his career, he’s one assist behind Duke great Bobby Hurley for the all-time record.</p><p>“We’ve got to feel this pain right now,” Michigan’s center Aday Mara said. “We’ve got to use this for the next month.”</p><p>Or maybe Michigan State will go the furthest. Or Nebraska. Or, ahem, Illinois. Look, that’s why they play the games. …</p><p>•<b> A BIG SALUTE</b> to Akron coach John Groce, whose Zips earned the No. 12 seed in the Midwest and will square off with Texas Tech in the first round. Underwood’s predecessor didn’t get it done at Illinois, but he’s absolutely crushing it in his return to the MAC, where he notched his fourth conference tournament title in the last five years and has a dangerous team with 18 wins in its last 19 games.</p>
entries.author: Steve Greenberg

entries.title: Horoscope for Monday, March 16, 2026
entries.summary: <h2>Moon Alert</h2> <p>Avoid shopping (except food and gas) or major decisions from 2:45 p.m. to 6:30 p.m. After that, the moon moves from Aquarius into Pisces.</p><h2>Aries (March 21-April 19)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Today you might meet someone unusual or avant-garde — someone who is different in some way. This person might be a new acquaintance, or they could be a member of a group. Be careful not to be influenced in a way you might later regret.</p><h2>Taurus (April 20-May 20)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Bosses or someone in a position of authority might do something that catches you off guard today. Whatever happens might instill a rebellious, independent reaction within you. You might rankle at restrictions from authority and want to be free.</p><h2>Gemini (May 21-June 20)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Travel might be a source of surprise for you today. You might have to travel when you didn't expect to do so, or, alternatively, travel plans might be delayed or canceled. News in the media might also be surprising. Meanwhile, you crave some excitement today — no question.</p><h2>Cancer (June 21-July 22)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Make friends with your bank account so you are not caught off guard by something. This is because issues related to shared property, banking, inheritances, wills, taxes and debt might hold a surprise for you today — it could be minor or major. Do your homework.</p><h2>Leo (July 23-Aug. 22)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Someone close to you, perhaps a partner, spouse or friend, might do or say something that catches you off guard today. It might be a real curveball. The thing to know is that you should avoid having a knee-jerk reaction to this. Take a moment so you can process things and figure out how you want to respond.</p><h2>Virgo (Aug. 23-Sept. 22)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Your work scene might suffer from interruptions today due to power outages, staff shortages, delayed deliveries and machinery breakdowns — including computers and technology. This can be frustrating. Pet owners should be vigilant because this is an accident-prone day for your pet.</p><h2>Libra (Sept. 23-Oct. 22)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Parents should know this is an accident-prone day for their kids, so keep an eye on them and know where they are at all times. (Remove any potential hazards if you can see them ahead of time.) Meanwhile, social occasions will surprise. You might receive an invitation, or conversely, an outing might be canceled or changed.</p><h2>Scorpio (Oct. 23-Nov. 21)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Your home routine might change today. Small appliances might break down, or a minor breakage could occur. Power outages are possible. Stock the fridge because surprise company might drop by. Do what you can to keep the family peace, especially if surprising information is revealed.</p><h2>Sagittarius (Nov. 22-Dec. 21)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Pay attention to everything you say and do today because this is a mildly accident-prone day for your sign. On the upside, however, you might have a brilliant idea that suddenly comes to you like an epiphany. Your daily routine will likely change.</p><h2>Capricorn (Dec. 22-Jan. 19)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Keep an eye on your money and possessions today because things are a bit erratic. You might find money; you might lose money. Do what you can to protect your belongings against loss, theft or damage.</p><h2>Aquarius (Jan. 20-Feb. 18)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Today, the moon is in your sign at odds with your ruler Uranus, which can make you hasty and ready to jump to conclusions. You feel rebellious and eager for excitement. Your personal freedom will be important.</p><h2>Pisces (Feb. 19-March 20)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> This is a strong day for you with the sun, retrograde Mercury and Mars in your sign. If you feel restless or something unusual suddenly occurs, you can take a moment to give everything a sober second thought.</p><h2>If today is your birthday</h2><p>Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney (1965) shares your birthday today. You are multitalented, visionary and realistic. You flourish where you can lead and inspire a team. This is a slower-paced year. Take time to rejuvenate your energy. Focus on business and personal relationships. Concentrate on your needs, and find relationships with people who have your back.</p>
entries.author: Georgia Nicols

entries.title: Watch out for these future pros in NCAA women's tournament
entries.summary: <p>The NCAA women’s basketball tournament kicks off this Wednesday, with UConn, UCLA, Texas, and South Carolina earning No. 1 seeds. For the Sky, who hold the No. 5 pick in the upcoming WNBA Draft, it’s a chance to watch some of the top prospects in the class.</p><p>Here are the Sun-Times’ players to watch, from potential No. 1 draft picks to potential fits for the Sky.</p><h3>Azzi Fudd, UConn (No. 1 seed)</h3><p>Unlike the last couple of years, there is no consensus No. 1 pick in the draft. But Fudd sits at the center of the debate. Some are calling her the best shooting prospect in history, after she made 44% of her 6.9 three-point attempts per game this season. If she goes No. 1, she will reunite with former UConn teammate Paige Bueckers on the Wings.</p><h3>Olivia Miles, TCU (No. 3 seed)</h3><p>Miles will enter the pros as one of the best passers in the league. At TCU, her team revolves around her. That’s partly why TCU fell to West Virginia in the Big 12 conference championship game: Miles ran into foul trouble early. Though she might not be drafted first overall, she won’t stray far from the top slot.</p><h3>Flau’jae Johnson, LSU (No. 2 seed)</h3><p>Johnson made it clear at the beginning of the season: she wants to be the No. 1 pick. At her best, her athleticism and shotmaking make her one of the most electric players in the game. But she didn’t play her best this season, sometimes fading into the background amid LSU’s talented lineup. That could benefit the Sky, who are looking for a player with star potential at No. 5.</p><h3>UCLA’s loaded starting five</h3><p>Five Bruins, five first-round draft prospects. Center Lauren Betts (6-7) looked like the No. 1 pick during her junior season, then doubts arose about her scoring against stiffer competition. But her defense should ensure she is a lottery pick. UCLA’s next three leading scorers are on the Sky’s radar: Kiki Rice, an explosive point guard; Gabriela Jaquez, a versatile wing; and Gianna Kneepkens, a lights-out shooter.</p><h3>Sarah Strong, UConn</h3><p>Strong is only a sophomore, but you don’t want to miss her ascent. UConn coach Geno Auriemma has already compared her to all-time great Huskies such as Maya Moore and Breanna Stewart.</p><p>“She’s like this mist that’s everywhere,” Auriemma said. “Your eyes just keep following her everywhere she goes, every play she makes. And you don’t say anything and you wonder, how can anything be that easy? How can anything look that easy?”<br></p>
entries.author: Alissa Hirsh

entries.title: Dear Abby: Woman told new romance needs to be kept a secret
entries.summary: <p><b>DEAR ABBY:</b> I was in a relationship with "John" for two years. Because he had nowhere to go, he continued living with me for six months after I ended the relationship. John has burned every bridge he's ever had, including his parents and his brother. He is an emotionally and verbally abusive alcoholic.</p><p>John's brother, "Jerry," and I have now fallen for each other. We grew up together and have been friends our entire lives. We have discussed that if it weren't for this issue, we would marry. My problem is Jerry is sure his mother will disapprove. He says he would end our relationship if they found out about us and she is against it.</p><p>We are almost 50 years old. I believe we could be "just friends" to the outside world, including Jerry's parents, and keep our private lives to ourselves for now. He is not concerned about what John would think. We have both experienced this type of connection only once before in our lives. How do we tell friends, family and, most importantly, his parents? — <i>FOUND THE ONE IN THE SOUTH</i></p><p><b>DEAR FOUND:</b> You and Jerry are nearly 50 years old. "We" shouldn't make any announcements to anyone. JERRY needs to be adult enough to summon up the courage to tell his mother he has fallen in love with you and plans to make you his wife. If he can't bring himself to do that, your relationship will eventually wither. Personally, I think it's time for you to take a step back so you won't be writing me about this problem a year or two or five from now. You deserve better treatment than this.</p><p><b>DEAR ABBY:</b> I have a daughter who resides in another state and has had self-confidence issues since she was young. Her first marriage ended when she found out her husband was having affairs. Her second husband appears to be controlling but gives her stability, which she likes. When I visit them, he's rude and condescending, which I have tried to ignore as he is her choice.</p><p>Over the last 20 years, he has become increasingly controlling and rude to the point that I no longer want to visit them. While I miss my daughter, I know she is aware and will do nothing about his behavior. We talk on the phone but only on speaker when he is present.</p><p>Now the conundrum: Do I leave a sizable inheritance to her, which I know he will control? While I don't want her to feel I am punishing her for her choice in a husband, it makes me feel like I would be rewarding him for his rude behavior. — <i>TORN DAD IN CALIFORNIA</i></p><p><b>DEAR DAD:</b> This is something you should discuss with your financial adviser. You do not have to reward her husband. You may be able to set up a trust for your daughter in which she receives a certain amount of money every month until her death. After that, the remainder of your estate might be left to a cause or charity of your choice.</p><p><i>Dear Abby is written by Abigail Van Buren, also known as Jeanne Phillips, and was founded by her mother, Pauline Phillips. Contact Dear Abby at www.DearAbby.com or P.O. Box 69440, Los Angeles, CA 90069.</i></p><p>To order "How to Write Letters for All Occasions," send your name and mailing address, plus check or money order for $8 (U.S. funds) to: Dear Abby — Letter Booklet, P.O. Box 446, Kings Mills, OH 45034-0446. (Shipping and handling are included in the price.)</p>
entries.author: Abigail Van Buren

entries.title: Mayor Johnson stripped neighborhoods of improvements by raiding TIFs
entries.summary: <p>If you live in a struggling Chicago neighborhood and you set aside a portion of your tax dollars to redevelop your neighborhood, you should be able to trust the city will use that money to improve your community.</p><p>That’s why the Illinois General Assembly created the <a class="Link" href="https://www.ilga.gov/Documents/legislation/ilcs/documents/006500050K11-74.4-3.htm" target="_blank" >tax increment financing</a> program. That’s why residents of these neighborhoods support them. That’s what the legislation we are championing in the General Assembly will spell out.</p><p>But that’s not what’s happening right now.</p><p>Mayor Brandon Johnson's city budget last year raided <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2025/10/21/brandon-johnson-2026-budget-chicago-record-tif-surplus" >$1 billion</a> from neighborhood TIF districts all over the city. The City Council revised the mayor's budget but kept his raids on TIFs. Here's what neighborhoods lost: $11 million from Humboldt Park; $5 million from 87th & Cottage Grove; $13 million from Belmont Central; $7 million from Englewood; $8 million from Galewood; $125 million from Pilsen.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-3b0000" name="module-3b0000"></a> <div class="RichTextSidebarModule-title">Commentary bug</div> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h2>Commentary</h2></div> </div><p>Residents and businesses in these neighborhoods dutifully paid into these TIF funds so that money could be used to bring economic development into these neighborhoods. They were not created to be slush funds that a mayor or council snatches when they're unwilling to look for efficiencies in the city budget and just need to plug a massive hole.</p><p>But those funds are now forever gone from Humboldt Park, Englewood, Burnside, Belmont-Central Galewood and Pilsen. Wondering why your property taxes are going up? Without the economic development these TIFs were supposed to bring, homeowners must make up the difference. Thanks to the billion-dollar raid, raising funds to develop these neighborhoods must now start all over again.</p><h3>Proposed law would put strict limits on TIF ‘cookie jar’</h3><p>So we have joined with state Sen. Bill Cunningham, D-Chicago, and state Rep. Bob Rita, D-Calumet City, to introduce legislation in the <a class="Link" href="https://ilga.gov/Legislation/BillStatus?DocNum=3236&GAID=18&DocTypeID=SB&LegId=165965&SessionID=114" target="_blank" >Illinois Senate</a> and in the <a class="Link" href="https://www.ilga.gov/documents/legislation/104/HB/PDF/10400HB4712lv.pdf" target="_blank" >Illinois House of Representatives</a> to prohibit mayors from raiding TIF funds to plug temporary budget holes.</p><p>These bills would limit a mayor’s ability to declare unspent TIF funds “surplus,” take them out of the neighborhoods and use them to boost spending at City Hall. This legislation will handcuff city officials tempted to plunder the TIF cookie jar by limiting them to taking only 5% of the TIF funds once every 10 years.</p><p>Here’s another shortsighted and outrageous part of raiding TIF funds: The city gets only <a class="Link" href="https://www.civicfed.org/understanding-chicagos-2026-record-tif-surplus" target="_blank" >23 cents on the dollar.</a> Seventy-seven cents of each of those dollars get sent back to other taxing bodies that didn’t even ask for them — the schools, Cook County government, the Metropolitan Water Reclamation District. Money raised in Englewood can be spent in Wilmette. Funds from the Pilsen TIF can be spent in Arlington Heights.</p><p>This legislation would stop this bad habit that successive Chicago mayors have engaged in. Other towns around Illinois have not abused TIF districts the way Chicago has. TIFs are a statewide program that have worked to revitalize blighted neighborhoods. Since our time working together at the Chicago Department of Planning and Development more than 20 years ago, we’ve seen how TIFs can be used to generate economic prosperity and transform neighborhoods.</p><h3>Used right, TIFs help economy</h3><p>TIFs used right can be a key component to growing our economy, and maintaining affordability and reasonable property tax rates for residents.</p><p>A TIF district caps the amount of property taxes that may be collected from homes and businesses in a district starting the year the TIF is formed. Taxes above that baseline amount are put into a fund for development projects for that district until the district sunsets in 23 years. Ideally, the improvements have made the district safer and less blighted, and allowed residents and businesses to flourish by the end of the TIF’s term.</p><p>TIFs, when used as intended, have great results. Look at the <a class="Link" href="https://www.englewoodsquare.com/" target="_blank" >Englewood Square</a> shopping center, <a class="Link" href="https://x.com/susanamendoza10/status/2001795184879697945" target="_blank" >built with TIF money</a> raised in Englewood, bringing a grocery store to what had been a food desert, a health clinic, a PNC bank branch, a Starbucks and a Chipotle. Look at the building going on in Roseland: stores, restaurants and amenities that make neighborhood living enjoyable.</p><p>My office collects data from TIF districts all over the state every year. We post the information on my website <a class="Link" href="http://www.illinoiscomptroller.gov/" target="_blank" >www.illinoiscomptroller.gov</a> for you to see and judge how the TIFs are working. You can be the judge: Are they living up to their potential?</p><p>Not if a mayor is going to empty them to plug one year’s budget hole.</p><p>Please encourage your state legislators to support these bills to keep neighborhood money in the neighborhoods and allow the TIF districts to function the way they were intended, breathing new life into struggling neighborhoods. This legislation might make life a little tougher for tomorrow’s mayors who won’t be able to raid TIF funds to plug budget holes. But Illinois’ cities and neighborhoods will be better off.</p><p><i>Susana Mendoza is the Illinois state comptroller. David Doig is president of Chicago Neighborhood Initiatives and formerly served as first deputy commissioner of planning in Chicago.</i></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-7a0000" name="module-7a0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody">Send letters to <a class="Link" href="mailto:letters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >letters@suntimes.com</a>. More about <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/submitting-opinion-and-letters" >how to submit here</a>.</div> </div><p></p>
entries.author: David Doig

entries.title: Local school council elections are upon us — Wednesday — a day after the Illinois primary
entries.summary: <p>The Illinois primary will be held Tuesday. The next day is another important day for Chicago residents to vote. That day, please visit your local school to vote in your local school council election.</p><p>Parents, learn about the candidates, and their priorities and visions. Community members, you can vote even if you’re not a parent. Support candidates who reflect what you care about in public education.</p><p>Chicago is world-famous for our politics — perhaps not always in a good way. But for many of our neighbors, LSC elections fly under the radar. They date back to the late 1980s, when then-Mayor Harold Washington led an effort to better share power among staff, families and neighborhoods in schools.</p><p>Every CPS school has an LSC, and every two years, Chicago undertakes one of the largest local elections in the country, where parents, staff, community members and students vote for representatives.</p><p>We are leaders of our neighborhood elementary school’s LSC. Working together, we are building a place where children thrive.</p><p>LSCs are elected bodies — mini school boards made up of parents, teachers, neighbors and students. They do not run schools directly but have real power and responsibility. LSCs vote on school improvement plans, approve school budgets, and evaluate or hire principals. They provide rare spaces to hear one another’s perspectives and strengthen school communities.</p><p>Coming out of the pandemic, we saw that children urgently needed support both emotionally and academically. We made these priorities in our school’s improvement plan, and voted to invest in social-emotional learning and academic intervention to help students recover.</p><p>Recently, student musicians performed a Women’s History Month rock show; eighth graders held a debate about the death penalty in literature class; and students stayed after school to rehearse for the spring play.</p><p>These came from LSC decisions to invest in after-school programs, arts positions, bilingual education and bringing parent mentors into classrooms.</p><p>No matter where you live in Chicago, you have a neighborhood school, and that means you have an LSC. Make sure you vote March 18!</p><p><i>Ben Dieterich, local school council chair, and Seth Lavin, principal, Brentano Math & Science Academy</i></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-bc0000" name="module-bc0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>Give us your take</h3><br>Send letters to the editor to <a class="Link" href="mailto:letters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >letters@suntimes.com</a>. To be considered for publication, letters must include your full name, your neighborhood or hometown and a phone number for verification purposes. Letters should be a maximum of approximately 375 words.</div> </div><p></p><h3><b>Nonviolence needs to be political ideal everywhere</b></h3><p>Democracy is in decline across the globe. Extremism is rising, strongman politics is gaining appeal, and disagreements too often harden into hatred. The warning signs are everywhere.</p><p>This moment calls us back to the moral tradition shared by Mahatma Gandhi, Martin Luther King Jr. and the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/politics/2026/03/06/rev-jesse-jackson-chicago-service-house-of-hope-pullman-neighborhood-barack-obama-jb-pritzker" >Rev. Jesse Jackson</a> — a lineage rooted in the power of disciplined nonviolence. Gandhi called his philosophy <a class="Link" href="https://www.britannica.com/topic/satyagraha-philosophy" target="_blank" >Satyagraha</a>, or truth-force: the belief that moral courage and collective action can confront injustice without reproducing cycles of violence.</p><p>Gandhi’s ideas did not remain confined to India. They helped shape the American Civil Rights Movement. King studied Gandhi’s strategy carefully and <a class="Link" href="https://kinginstitute.stanford.edu/india-trip" target="_blank" >visited India in 1959</a>, later calling him <a class="Link" href="https://www.politico.com/story/2013/03/mahatma-gandhis-lightguided-martin-luther-king-jr-088581" target="_blank" >“the guiding light of our technique of nonviolent social change.”</a></p><p>Chicago became an important center of that tradition. For decades, Jackson — King’s protégé — expanded the practice of nonviolence beyond protest. Through coalition-building and economic justice advocacy, Jackson showed that love and democracy must be defended in the streets and within institutions of power.</p><p>Today, the stakes are global. Even in India, the land Gandhi helped liberate, extremist ideologies have gained ground. Around the world, including in the U.S., democratic norms are being tested by intolerance, disinformation and the influence of political funding.</p><p>Chicago's <a class="Link" href="https://www.sacreddemocracy.org/" target="_blank" >South Asian American Coalition to Renew Democracy Acts</a>, or SACRED Acts, has taken up this charge locally. As part of the Season for Nonviolence, it launched a pledge urging candidates and elected officials to reject financial support from organizations tied to extremist, caste-supremacist or hate-based ideologies.</p><p>At a public news conference at which I delivered the keynote, 50-plus local leaders joined my call to reject funding from anti-democratic groups like the <a class="Link" href="https://www.npr.org/2019/05/03/706808616/the-powerful-group-shaping-the-rise-of-hindu-nationalism-in-india" target="_blank" >Rashtriya Swayamsevak Sangh</a>, a right-wing paramilitary group that planned the assassination of my great-grandfather. While I feel disappointment at prominent leaders who did not sign on, those who did sign on reaffirmed their commitment to expose money trails that undermine democratic values — whether in India or the U.S.</p><p>This effort reflects the same moral lineage that shaped Gandhi, King and Jackson: the belief that nonviolence is not only a personal ethic but a public responsibility. Nonviolence or nonexistence is not merely a slogan. It is a choice about the kind of democracy we will build — and whether we will defend it together.</p><p><i>Tushar Gandhi, founder, Mahatma Gandhi Foundation, Mumbai, India</i></p><h3>‘Nothing left to target’</h3><p>Dear President Trump: I heard on CNN last week that you said, via <a class="Link" href="https://www.axios.com/2026/03/11/trump-iran-war-end-withdrawal" target="_blank" >Axios</a>, there was "practically nothing left" to target in Iran.</p><p>When I was in Vietnam in 1969, at age 19, I suggested to our top sergeant the way to end the war was to bomb the country, grid square by grid square, until there would be nothing left to target.</p><p>We could have used you to end that war, six years before it actually ended. Oh, I forgot: bone spurs. Five cases of them. It must have been tough to live with them.</p><p><i>Calogero Lombardo, Old Irving Park</i></p>
entries.author: Letters to the Editor

entries.title: On average, an L rider gets shoved onto the tracks from CTA platforms every month
entries.summary: <p>A 59-year-old River Forest man walked from his home to the Harlem Avenue L stop on the morning of Dec. 1 to take the train to Rush University Medical Center, where he volunteered when not working his regular job as a grocery clerk at Jewel.</p><p>Intellectually disabled, living independently and described as a “good, generous human being,” the man relied on the CTA to get around the Chicago area because he didn’t drive.</p><p>But what should’ve been a routine trip seven stops east along the Blue Line from Forest Park to the West Side nearly turned fatal for the man, who was shoved off the commuter platform onto the tracks by a suspect with a long rap sheet — who badgered the victim for $1 and apparently got upset when he didn’t get it.</p><p>“He fell approximately eight to 10 feet,” said one of the victim’s two sisters, both of whom spoke to the Chicago Sun-Times but asked that neither they nor their brother be publicly named.</p><p>Part of the man’s right leg was shattered while his left leg also sustained a fracture, and he landed near the third rail that powers trains and pulses with 600 volts of electricity.</p><p>“Had he touched that, it’d be a much different story,” the second sister said. “We’re very grateful that didn’t happen.”</p><p>A Sun-Times analysis found the man is far from the only L rider shoved onto train tracks in recent years. More than 60 instances have been recorded in CTA paperwork since 2021 — amounting to one incident a month, on average.</p><p>The records don’t show anybody dying from being pushed during that time frame, but there were injuries and lots of close calls — and in past years, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2020/4/16/21224633/fajour-hodges-2nd-charged-murder-ex-marine-mamadou-balde-pushed-cta-train-jackson-ryan-munn" target="_blank" >there have been fatalities</a>.</p><p>In the Harlem Avenue incident, a train was nearby, but the operator luckily saw the victim on the tracks south of the platform and was able to stop an estimated 100 feet short of the fallen man, according to records and interviews. The power was cut off — routine procedure in such emergencies.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-770000" name="image-770000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" id="rte-96627500-1d9a-11f1-acf4-0dda1151c703"><li>On April 27, 2025, a woman at the Red Line’s 63rd Street L stop was sprayed with an “unknown substance” and shoved onto the train tracks while the offender took off running down the Dan Ryan Expressway.</li><li>A week earlier at the Howard Street station that serves as a link between the Purple, Red and Yellow lines, a man dressed in all red began inexplicably “undressing on the platform,” spurring another passenger to kick “the indecently exposed offender, causing him to fall.”</li><li>Amid an apparent “domestic dispute” at the Blue Line’s Kedzie/Homan stop in 2021, a man pushed a woman onto the tracks.</li><li>This past August at the Blue Line’s Pulaski stop, “two youth offenders” were seen “fighting with an older male on the platform in an attempt to rob him,” records show. They “then pushed” him “to track level.”</li><li>At the Blue Line’s Belmont L station in April 2024, a robbery took a different turn, as a man fell onto the tracks “after being punched by a victim that he was trying to rob.”</li></ul><p>Most of the incidents appeared intentional, sometimes involving people with apparent mental health issues, sometimes the outcome of another crime, such as a stick-up or a fight, records show.</p><p>One exception occurred on Aug. 20, 2024, at the Howard Street stop when a 69-year-old rider was pushed onto the tracks — by a security guard’s dog.</p><p>“The passenger walked by the security dog too close and startled it,” CTA records say.</p><p>“When the dog jumped up, the security guard lost his balance, causing him to lose his grip on the leash, resulting in the dog pushing the passenger to the right of way.”</p><p>A worker “assisted the passenger back to the platform... the passenger declined medical assistance, however he dropped his shoe and cell phone on the right of way.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-8e0001"
