alt="The Howard Street L station, where a security dog knocked a rider onto the tracks several years ago." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d799cba/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff0%2F74%2Fbbc525974cbcafef3e2275f16b7b%2Fwatchpush-260315-23.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/b783ea9/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff0%2F74%2Fbbc525974cbcafef3e2275f16b7b%2Fwatchpush-260315-23.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d799cba/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff0%2F74%2Fbbc525974cbcafef3e2275f16b7b%2Fwatchpush-260315-23.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>The Howard Street L station, where a security dog knocked a rider onto the tracks several years ago.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere / Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The Trump administration has been threatening to withhold federal funding from the CTA if the agency doesn’t <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/transportation/2026/03/10/chicago-transit-authority-cta-crime-security-plan-fta" target="_blank" >come up with a better plan to deal</a> with violence on public transportation. <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/transportation/2026/03/10/chicago-transit-authority-cta-crime-security-plan-fta" target="_blank" ></a></p><p>While President Donald Trump’s often-aggressive policies toward major U.S. cities are sometimes viewed as a cynical means to undercut and destabilize Democratic power centers, safety and security indeed are troublesome on the transit system — which ultimately falls under the oversight of Mayor Brandon Johnson.</p><p>The Sun-Times analysis <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2026/03/02/chicago-cta-transit-crime-assault-battery-leerhsen" target="_blank" >comes not long after the newspaper found</a> aggravated assaults and batteries on the CTA reached a 24-year high in 2025, with those crimes also up so far in 2026 compared to the same period a year earlier.</p><p>For decades, the CTA has faced criticism about crime, and the agency has tried to emphasize that in relative terms, the system is safe given how many riders it has. But leaders have at times seemed to prioritize cost-savings over safety — while increasingly relying on security technology that may help catch offenders but can’t intervene when a rider is being mugged or beaten.</p><p>In New York City, riders shoved onto tracks in the subway system has been almost epidemic, and that has played into the main transit agency’s decision to start installing barriers on platforms.</p><p>“Being pushed in front of a train is among the greatest fears of city dwellers,” The New York Times wrote in February, detailing the ordeal of a man who had been pushed onto the tracks on Dec. 31, 2024, and has sued New York’s main transit agency for “gross negligence” and “reckless conduct.”</p><p>Just days ago, two passengers were shoved onto tracks in New York in what was described as an unprovoked attack. One of the victims was in his 80s.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-320000" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/1ab33ed/2147483647/strip/true/crop/640x359+0+60/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd8%2Fd7%2Fba1c25d84bb5a4592c66e24dc3d4%2Fimage-72-copy.jpg" alt="A platform barrier at one of Tokyo's commuter train stations." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/1ab33ed/2147483647/strip/true/crop/640x359+0+60/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd8%2Fd7%2Fba1c25d84bb5a4592c66e24dc3d4%2Fimage-72-copy.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c5ef70c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/640x359+0+60/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd8%2Fd7%2Fba1c25d84bb5a4592c66e24dc3d4%2Fimage-72-copy.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/1ab33ed/2147483647/strip/true/crop/640x359+0+60/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd8%2Fd7%2Fba1c25d84bb5a4592c66e24dc3d4%2Fimage-72-copy.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>A platform barrier at one of Tokyo’s commuter train stations.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Roger Grabowski</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Overseas, train systems in places such as Tokyo also have different kinds of protective barriers on platforms, though they appear to be primarily aimed at preventing accidental falls as well as suicides.</p><p>Other findings based on the CTA records of people being pushed onto train tracks show:<br></p><ul class="rte2-style-ul" id="rte-96627501-1d9a-11f1-acf4-0dda1151c703"><li>Most incidents happened on the Red and Blue lines, the CTA’s only 24-hour routes, followed by the Green Line.</li><li>Overall, a dozen or so incidents happened in 2025, while a similar number of shoving occurrences were reported in 2024.</li><li>The vast majority of incidents involved male victims and male assailants.</li></ul><p>Neither a 2022 incident involving an L motorman shoved onto the tracks at the Red Line’s Granville stop nor another shoving incident on the Blue Line that same year were included in the records released by the CTA, which apparently does not track shoving incidents in a specific crime category — and so doesn't keep a comprehensive list.</p><p>The Dec. 1 incident at the Harlem stop also wasn’t in the records, which the Sun-Times requested under the state's open records law.</p><p>Acting CTA President Nora Leerhsen wouldn’t speak to the Sun-Times, but her office released a statement that said in part:</p><p>"Random, unprovoked incidents of people being pushed onto CTA tracks are extremely rare. In fact, the 66 incidents in the data cited occurred during a five-year period where CTA provided more than a <b>half-billion</b> train rides. None of those incidents resulted in death and the vast majority of them were the result of a verbal or physical altercation getting out of hand."</p><p>"That said, CTA takes every incident seriously — and we are always concerned with the impact these incidents have on the people involved, our employees and the thousands of riders affected by service disruptions. That’s why CTA increased the number of law enforcement hours on its system by 75 percent as part of the March 2026 Security Enhancement Plan."</p><p>"CTA has met with the MTA" — New York's transit agency — "to learn about their physical platform barrier initiatives — stationary fencing in stations. The agency is also evaluating the feasibility of piloting a similar solution at its rail stations based on what its learned from peer agencies."</p><p>The CTA also has been testing technology to better detect people or objects on tracks "and enable swift intervention," according to the agency, saying the technology involves cameras and artificial intelligence, and fiber optic cables.</p><p>"CTA is not relying on any single solution. It is addressing both the technical gaps and the underlying social issues that can lead to these rare but serious incidents."</p><p>According to one of the sisters of the Dec. 1 victim, their brother “was going down the ramp” around 8:30 a.m. when “this person asked him for a dollar, and he said, ‘I don’t have a dollar.’”</p><p>The assailant “continued to harass” the victim onto the platform, where he “eventually pushed him off."</p><p>The alleged offender, Tommie O. Carter, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2025/12/03/tommie-carter-cta-blue-line-charges" target="_blank" >was quickly arrested</a> and has been charged with, among other offenses, attempted murder. He is being held in Cook County Jail and declined through the sheriff’s office to comment. He has a lengthy arrest history.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-9a0000" name="image-9a0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/097df4f/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1150x645+0+141/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ffd%2Ffd%2F8e86150d49ff9d43eaa91b99ee27%2Fscreenshot-2026-03-11-at-6-12-42-pm.png 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/cfed20e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1150x645+0+141/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ffd%2Ffd%2F8e86150d49ff9d43eaa91b99ee27%2Fscreenshot-2026-03-11-at-6-12-42-pm.png 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/36592a4/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1150x645+0+141/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ffd%2Ffd%2F8e86150d49ff9d43eaa91b99ee27%2Fscreenshot-2026-03-11-at-6-12-42-pm.png" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Tommie O. Carter, who was arrested for shoving the 59-year-old River Forest man onto the tracks Dec. 1 at the Blue Line's Harlem L stop." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/36592a4/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1150x645+0+141/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ffd%2Ffd%2F8e86150d49ff9d43eaa91b99ee27%2Fscreenshot-2026-03-11-at-6-12-42-pm.png 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/6f51942/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1150x645+0+141/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ffd%2Ffd%2F8e86150d49ff9d43eaa91b99ee27%2Fscreenshot-2026-03-11-at-6-12-42-pm.png 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/36592a4/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1150x645+0+141/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ffd%2Ffd%2F8e86150d49ff9d43eaa91b99ee27%2Fscreenshot-2026-03-11-at-6-12-42-pm.png" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Tommie O. Carter, who was arrested for shoving the 59-year-old River Forest man onto the tracks Dec. 1 at the Blue Line’s Harlem L stop.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Cook County sheriff</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The victim’s sisters say their brother was transported to Loyola University Medical Center in Maywood and has undergone two leg surgeries. He is now staying at a physical rehabilitation center where only recently he was able to take some steps with a walker.</p><p>The sisters said it would be easy to be consumed by anger over the incident, so they’re trying to stay positive. But it’s clear the suspect “should’ve never been on the street,” one of them said.</p><p>The other sister said Carter “needs to sit for a long time and think about what he’s done.”</p><p>She said of her brother: “He’s one of the physically and mentally strongest people I know ... his spirits continue to be very good ... but the big question for us is we don't know what his new normal is going to be."</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2025/11/03/chicago-transit-authority-subway-surfing-new-york-cta-mta" target="_blank" >CTA hopes this kind of rush hour — subway surfing — doesn’t take root here as it has in New York</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>
entries.author: David Struett

entries.title: Chicago and suburban Democratic voters more likely to vote for candidates who fought ICE, poll finds
entries.summary: <p>Democratic and independent voters still see immigration enforcement as a top issue in Chicago and the surrounding suburbs — and are more likely to support a candidate who protested President Donald Trump’s "Operation Midway Blitz," a new poll commissioned by a progressive coalition found.</p><p>Several candidates in congressional and state races have made their anti-Immigration and Customs Enforcement stance a focal point of their campaigns, including the top U.S. Senate Democratic candidates. Tuesday’s primary elections will serve as a test of whether campaign messaging about fighting ICE worked — and how views on immigration have changed during Trump’s second term.</p><p>When voters were asked about ICE, 63% had an unfavorable view, while 31% had a favorable view. That broke down sharply by political parties, with 90% of Democrats viewing ICE unfavorably compared to 9% of Republicans. Among independents, the number was 62%. </p><p>In Chicago, 75% of those polled overall had an unfavorable view of ICE. In the rest of Cook County, 62% of those polled viewed ICE unfavorably, and in surrounding counties, 56% had an unfavorable view of ICE. </p><p>About 70% of likely Democratic primary voters who participated in the poll said a candidate’s position on ICE was the most important issue or very important to how they’d cast their vote. Nearly two-thirds of respondents said they’d be more likely to support a candidate who has been active in anti-ICE protests.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/elections/2026/graphics/voter-guide-primary-election-illinois/" >2026 Illinois Primary Elections Voter Guide</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>About 14% of participants said they resisted ICE actions themselves, and about a quarter of the others said they wished they could have done more. Those who took action were more likely to be younger Chicago residents who consider themselves liberal voters.</p><p>Those actions included signing anti-ICE petitions (32%), contacting local officials (27%), joining vigils or protests (24%), tracking ICE locally (16%) and blowing whistles to alert their neighbors to ICE presence (11%).</p><p>The survey of 800 registered voters in the Chicago media market, including Cook, DeKalb, DuPage, Kane, Kankakee, Kendall, McHenry and Will counties, was taken March 1-4. Interviews were conducted by live phone calls and text-to-web responses. According to the pollster, self-described Democrats outnumbered Republicans in the sample 44% to 22%. Another 33% identifying as independent voters.</p><p>Of those polled, 33% approved of the job Trump is doing as president, while 64% disapproved. On Trump's handling of immigration, 35% approved, while 63% said they disapproved.</p><p>The poll was commissioned by Hands Off Chicago, a coalition of progressive community groups including the Illinois Coalition for Immigrant and Refugee Rights, Equality Illinois and the Chicago Federation of Labor. The group also conducted four in-person focus groups — two with white progressives and liberals, one with Latinas and another with Black men. They also conducted an online focus group with swing voters.</p><p>Exposure to ICE played a role in voters’ reaction to the agency, with 73% of those who saw raids in their neighborhoods holding an unfavorable view of the agency. The number declined for those who didn’t have raids in their neighborhood, with 51% of those voters viewing ICE unfavorably.</p><p>The poll also found that 29% of Latinos said they saw ICE agents in their communities personally, more than twice the rate of Black or white voters.</p><p>About 75% of poll respondents said immigration is good for the country, with 83% saying they support providing a path to citizenship for people in the U.S. without documentation.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2026/01/31/brandon-johnson-investigate-ice-prosecution-executive-order-chicago" >Mayor Brandon Johnson orders CPD to investigate federal immigration agents for potential prosecution</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>At least 2,800 people were arrested in Illinois during "Operation Midway Blitz" between mid-September through mid-October, primarily in the Chicago area, according to ICE data obtained by the Data Deportation Project and statements about Border Patrol arrests made by the Justice Department in court. On Sept. 12, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2025/11/17/silverio-villegas-gonzalez-ice-dhs-trump-midway-blitz-shooting-homicide-franklin-park-chicago" target="_blank" >Silverio Villegas González,</a> a father and cook originally from Mexico, was fatally shot at “close range” as he allegedly tried to flee a traffic stop. Operation Midway Blitz slowed in mid-November.</p>
entries.author: Mitchell Armentrout

entries.title: Review: ‘White Rooster’ is fun, ambitious and insistently intriguing even if it doesn’t quite make sense
entries.summary: <p>Narrative clarity is not the strength of the unique and wildly evocative “White Rooster,” a fanciful, fun, extremely theatrical mashup of ghost stories, Chinese folklore, the mythology of the American West, rock music, puppetry, expressive movement, earthy dark humor and dreamy illogic.</p><p>From the creative mind of writer/director Matthew C. Yee (the 2023 musical “Lucy and Charlie’s Honeymoon”) and receiving its world premiere at Lookingglass Theatre, “White Rooster” comes off as so energetic, well-performed and insistently intriguing that its sensual qualities provide an enjoyable vibe even if it never quite makes coherent sense.</p><p>Yee toys from the start with archetypes he filters through an oddball lens. The show seems set in an American, post-Gold-Rush era “ghost town.” That term has a double entendre here: Not only is the town defined by deprivation — the mines have long since been emptied and become unstable — but it is also literally populated with ghosts. The opening musical sequence — the show isn’t a musical, but frequent, partial songs, backed by electric guitar, serve as a form of third-person narration — informs us that “There’s ghosts in this town, they stop and say hi/But no one can tell if they’re dead or alive.”</p><div class="RatingCard Enhancement" data-module data-rating="3.5" data-align-center> <div class="RatingCard-title"> ‘White Rooster’ </div> <div class="RatingCard-content"> <span class="RatingCard-star"> <span class="RatingCard-star-full"> <svg class=""> <use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use> </svg> </span> <span class="RatingCard-star-half"> <svg class=""> <use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use> </svg> </span> </span> <span class="RatingCard-star"> <span class="RatingCard-star-full"> <svg class=""> <use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use> </svg> </span> <span class="RatingCard-star-half"> <svg class=""> <use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use> </svg> </span> </span> <span class="RatingCard-star"> <span class="RatingCard-star-full"> <svg class=""> <use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use> </svg> </span> <span class="RatingCard-star-half"> <svg class=""> <use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use> </svg> </span> </span> <span class="RatingCard-star"> <span class="RatingCard-star-full"> <svg class=""> <use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use> </svg> </span> <span class="RatingCard-star-half"> <svg class=""> <use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use> </svg> </span> </span> </div> </div><p></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-bc0000" name="module-bc0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><b>When:</b> Through April 26<br><b>Where:</b> Lookingglass Theatre, 821 N. Michigan Ave.<br><b>Tickets:</b> $40-$89<br><b>Info:</b> <a class="Link" href="https://lookingglasstheatre.org/plays/white-rooster/" target="_blank" >lookingglasstheatre.org</a><br><b>Run time:</b> 2 hours and 15 minutes with one intermission<br></div> </div><p>Throughout, Yee allows the characters to acknowledge the combination of the literal and the fantastic in a humorously self-conscious way. “Are you a ghost?” asks lead ingenue Min (Sunnie Eraso) in the opening scene of the woman June (Noelle Oh) living in her attic, followed by “Sorry. Can I ask that? Is that rude?”</p><p>In the core narrative, Min falls for a young man named Pong (Reilly Oh), who then, along with Min’s father, John, (Mark Montgomery) dies in a mine collapse. Pong’s grandparents (Louise Lamson and Daniel Lee Smith) suggest Min marry Pong — “Unmarried and dead. Bad combo. Ask anyone,” explains the caring and wise granddad.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/theater/2026/03/13/chicago-theater-lookingglass-white-rooster-playwright-matthew-yee-chinese-culture-history" target="_blank" ><u>Yee has discussed that the play</u></a> was inspired by his own family history of such a Chinese “ghost marriage,” with the spirit of the dead spouse represented physically by a white rooster. So yes, Min marries a rooster. “You may kiss the bird,” says the highly quirky holy man (Elliot Esquivel), pointing to the puppet title fowl at the culmination of the wedding.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-560000" name="image-560000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/cc40738/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7275x4083+0+385/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F9d%2Fb2%2F5b3d25e9407a8e43638ea7532b7f%2Fdaniel-lee-smith-louise-lamson-elliot-eqsuivel-sunnie-eraso-photo-by-justin-barbin.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a90de65/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7275x4083+0+385/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F9d%2Fb2%2F5b3d25e9407a8e43638ea7532b7f%2Fdaniel-lee-smith-louise-lamson-elliot-eqsuivel-sunnie-eraso-photo-by-justin-barbin.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/36bca96/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7275x4083+0+385/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F9d%2Fb2%2F5b3d25e9407a8e43638ea7532b7f%2Fdaniel-lee-smith-louise-lamson-elliot-eqsuivel-sunnie-eraso-photo-by-justin-barbin.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Daniel Lee Smith, Louise Lamson, Elliot Eqsuivel, Sunnie Eraso. Photo by Justin Barbin.jpg" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/36bca96/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7275x4083+0+385/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F9d%2Fb2%2F5b3d25e9407a8e43638ea7532b7f%2Fdaniel-lee-smith-louise-lamson-elliot-eqsuivel-sunnie-eraso-photo-by-justin-barbin.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/3e2b201/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7275x4083+0+385/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F9d%2Fb2%2F5b3d25e9407a8e43638ea7532b7f%2Fdaniel-lee-smith-louise-lamson-elliot-eqsuivel-sunnie-eraso-photo-by-justin-barbin.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/36bca96/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7275x4083+0+385/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F9d%2Fb2%2F5b3d25e9407a8e43638ea7532b7f%2Fdaniel-lee-smith-louise-lamson-elliot-eqsuivel-sunnie-eraso-photo-by-justin-barbin.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Daniel Lee Smith, Louise Lamson, Elliot Esquivel and Sunnie Eraso star in “White Rooster.” </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Justin Barbin</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>But any foregrounded storytelling here competes for attention with a series of background stories that pile up. Characters, such as Min’s mother (Karen Aldridge), gleefully grind any forward action to a halt to tell another poetically creepy story, such as a miner who couldn’t stop digging until the mountain rebelled, a town bedeviled by a drought demon and zombies, a man whose pet pig eats gold, etc.</p><p>These tales are told through a mix of shadow and physical puppetry. Puppet designer Caitlin McLeod’s excellent work defines much of the world, along with a bare wooden-plank floor and configurable, appropriately ghostly white curtains dominating Natsu Onoda Power’s set, and eclectic costumes from Mara Blumenfeld that suggest period but with a heavy tilt toward the bohemian chic.</p><p>Like so many actors who start to write and direct, Yee has created a show where performers can shine, and the cast here certainly does. There’s a wonderful natural present-ness that the actors bring to the weirdness of this world that lets the audience settle in. Although all have extremely enjoyable moments, Aldridge, Esquivel and Smith are provided with the most opportunity to exaggerate entertainingly in a way that still feels grounded in the work’s strange reality.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-d10000" name="image-d10000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/03adf46/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7927x4449+0+419/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb3%2F1a%2Fdc7a257a414db5bddee9fcb1bd5c%2Fnoelle-oh-reilly-oh-sunnie-eraso-photo-by-justin-barbin.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/b29c352/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7927x4449+0+419/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb3%2F1a%2Fdc7a257a414db5bddee9fcb1bd5c%2Fnoelle-oh-reilly-oh-sunnie-eraso-photo-by-justin-barbin.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/11cc7fd/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7927x4449+0+419/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb3%2F1a%2Fdc7a257a414db5bddee9fcb1bd5c%2Fnoelle-oh-reilly-oh-sunnie-eraso-photo-by-justin-barbin.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Noelle Oh, Reilly Oh, Sunnie Eraso. Photo by Justin Barbin.jpg" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/11cc7fd/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7927x4449+0+419/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb3%2F1a%2Fdc7a257a414db5bddee9fcb1bd5c%2Fnoelle-oh-reilly-oh-sunnie-eraso-photo-by-justin-barbin.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/3e07184/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7927x4449+0+419/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb3%2F1a%2Fdc7a257a414db5bddee9fcb1bd5c%2Fnoelle-oh-reilly-oh-sunnie-eraso-photo-by-justin-barbin.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/11cc7fd/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7927x4449+0+419/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb3%2F1a%2Fdc7a257a414db5bddee9fcb1bd5c%2Fnoelle-oh-reilly-oh-sunnie-eraso-photo-by-justin-barbin.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Noelle Oh, Reilly Oh and Sunnie Eraso star in “White Rooster.” Reilly portrays Pong, a young man who dies in a mine collapse and whose spirit is married to Min (Eraso).</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Justin Barbin</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Yee starts to merge the present story with all the background ghost stories, to the point that characters even change names. It can certainly be hard to follow, and in truth, I’m still puzzled over whether its surreality has a meaningful logic underneath it.</p><p>And it’s certainly fair to ask what it all means. I appreciate the fact that there is no moral here — this is not an Aesop-style fable with a lesson to be learned. But its obsessions ring true in the way that abstract stories do — it’s about people who dream and strive and fail and grieve and struggle to understand, and how both the past and the imagination constantly impede the present so-called reality. Yee’s mix of Asian and American genres here, and the willingness to take none of them too seriously, expresses an embrace of multiple storytelling traditions while also seeking some deeply personal way of explaining the world.</p><p>Pruning and sharpening should most certainly continue on “White Rooster,” but this type of ambitious artistic endeavor deserves all sorts of encouragement no matter how messy its current state.</p>
entries.author: Steven Oxman | For the Sun-Times

entries.title: Chiefs might have found temporary replacement for Patrick Mahomes in former Bears QB Justin Fields: report
entries.summary: <p>Former <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears" target="_blank" >Bears</a> first-round pick Justin Fields is expected to get an intriguing opportunity with the Chiefs as he continues to trying to prove himself as a starting quarterback. The Jets were finalizing a trade Monday to send him to Kansas City for a 2027 sixth-round pick, ESPN reported, and he could get a chance to start as Patrick Mahomes works back from a torn ACL.</p><p>As the team waits for Mahomes, who is in question for the start of the season, Fields will be by far their most experienced quarterback with 53 starts in five seasons. The Chiefs’ other quarterbacks are Chris Oladokun (two starts) and Jake Haener (one).</p><p>Fields, 27, will be on his third team in three years since the Bears moved on from him to draft Caleb Williams in 2024.</p><p>He signed a two-year, $40 million deal with the Jets as a free agent a year ago, but played just nine games due to injury and poor performance. He completed 62.7% of his passes, averaged 139.9 yards per game, threw seven touchdown passes and one interception for an 89.5 passer rating, which was second-highest of his career. He also rushed for 383 yards and four touchdowns.</p><p>Former Bears general manager Ryan Pace traded a 2022 first-round pick to the Giants to move up and draft Fields 11th overall in 2021. Successor Ryan Poles then <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2024/03/16/justin-fields-trade-chicago-bears-ryan-poles-draft-pick-caleb-williams-pittsburgh-steelers-stats" >dealt him to the Steelers for a sixth-round pick in 2024</a>, and Fields played decently enough that season to earn a major payday from the Jets.</p><p>The Bears bungled Fields’ onboarding as a rookie, and he never turned the corner for them other than becoming one of the most dangerous running quarterbacks in the NFL. He had an 82.3 passer rating and went 10-28 as a starter in three seasons for the Bears.</p>
entries.author: Jason Lieser

entries.title: Mayor Brandon Johnson answers your questions on In The Loop with Sasha-Ann Simons
entries.summary: <p>Mayor Brandon Johnson returns to WBEZ 91.5 at 9 a.m. Monday to answer your questions live on the morning talk show “In the Loop” with host Sasha-Ann Simons. Call 866-915-WBEZ to ask the mayor a question.</p><p>Last month, Johnson talked about an executive order aiming to create new ways to hold U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement agents accountable for alleged wrongdoing.</p><p>"The state’s attorney has made it clear she would prosecute criminal behavior if there was evidence brought to her," Johnson said. "So now there is a clear directive that I’ve provided to my police department to ensure that evidence is not just collected, but it’s preserved."</p><p>Amy in the Jefferson Park neighborhood was worried Johnson’s rhetoric would encourage the Trump administration to send ICE agents back to Chicago. Johnson responded by saying, "This is everything about what this president is determined to do, which is undermine our democracy. ...This is not a challenge to the president; this is a challenge to us as people who define this country through a very fundamental value, the ability to have free and fair elections and to be able to have communities and cities that are safe and vibrant. We should not be afraid of our own government."</p><p>Johnson has previously answered listener questions about community resources for kids, job opportunities for Black Chicagoans, teen curfew concerns and locking horns with City Council over the city budget.</p><p><i>WBEZ’s “In the Loop” connects you with the people behind the stories: experts, neighbors and newsmakers who shape the city we share. For more on “In the Loop” and to listen to their segments, visit their website. You can also listen to the show on Spotify or the WBEZ app.</i></p>
entries.author: Sasha-Ann Simons | WBEZ

entries.title: Over 350 flights canceled at O'Hare as wintry conditions create hazardous morning commute
entries.summary: <p>Snow and icy conditions have sparked several traffic crashes and made for a treacherous morning commute Monday. </p><p>Sunday's heavy rain turned to sleet overnight and then snow which was expected to fall on-and-off throughout the day, weather officials said. </p><p>"Just take it slow, make sure you give yourself plenty of time to make it to where you need to be," said Rafal Ogorek, a meteorologist with the weather service. "Also be prepared for potentially encountering sharp visibility reductions."</p><p>A winter weather advisory was in effect for Cook, Will, Grundy and Livingston counties until 1 p.m., according to the weather service. A blizzard warning was in effect until the same time for Boone, DeKalb, Lee, Ogle, Winnebago and McHenry counties.</p><p>O'Hare recorded 1.1 inches of snow while Midway saw 1.6 inches, according to the weather service. Romeoville measured 2 inches of snow and most of the Chicago area got 1-to-2 inches of snow as of 7 a.m. </p><p>About a tenth-of-an-inch to an inch of snow was expected throughout the rest of the day, forecasters said. </p><p>Over 340 flights were canceled at O'Hare Airport Monday as wintry conditions caused slick roads and reduced visibility impacting morning commuters.</p><p>As of 7:15 a.m., over 350 flights were canceled while at least another 150 were delayed, according to<a class="Link" href="https://www.flightaware.com/live/cancelled/today/KORD" target="_blank" >FlightAware</a>. At Midway, at least 20 flights were canceled while another 90 were delayed.</p><p>State troopers responded to 38 property damage crashes, 10 crashes involving injuries and 34 calls for assistance between midnight and 7 a.m., according to Illinois state police.</p><p>Monday's wintry conditions are part of a "<a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/weather/2026/03/15/50-mph-wind-thunderstorms-chicago" target="_blank" >strong spring storm</a>" that brought 1.76 inches of precipitation to O'Hare Sunday, making it the wettest March 15 at the airport, according to the National Weather Service. The previous record was 1.43 inches set in 1943.</p><p>A strong low pressure storm system moving east was responsible for the rain, winds and snow passing through the Chicago area. The latest stretch of storms came shortly after <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/weather/2026/03/12/tornadoes-weather-illinois-chicago-suburbs-indiana-hail-storm-flooding" target="_blank" >destructive tornadoes</a> and <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/weather/2026/03/13/winds-nearing-60-mph-may-knock-down-trees-power-lines-and-make-driving-very-difficult-in-chicago-area" target="_blank" >high speed wind gusts</a> impacted the area last week. </p><p>Temperatures were expected to be in the mid-20s Monday with strong winds gusting up to 40 mph creating single digit wind chills, forecasters said. Similar, though less windy conditions with highs also in the 20s were forecasted Tuesday before temperatures return to the 40s and 50s later in the week.</p><p>"It's going to feel a lot more like winter," Ogorek said.</p><p>"Mid-March tends to be a time of year where we can have when we can experience a wide range of temperatures, so sometimes it'll be it'll feel more like late spring and other times it'll feel more like winter, Ogorek said. "We just happen to be in a period of time right now where we'll be experiencing the colder end of the temperature spectrum."</p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Death of woman, 84, assaulted in Morgan Park home ruled homicide
entries.summary: <p>Police are investigating the Friday death of an 84-year-old woman in the Morgan Park neighborhood as a homicide, officials said. </p><p>Belinda Ruiz's son found Belina Ruiz "cold to the touch" around 8:45 a.m. Friday inside her home on the front room floor at 11000 S. Washtenaw Ave. according to Chicago police reports. </p><p>Responding paramedics said Ruiz died from apparently natural causes and she was pronounced dead at the scene at 9:12 a.m. Friday, according to police reports. </p><p>But an autopsy determined Ruiz's death was a homicide due to multiple injuries from an assault, the Cook County medical examiner's office said. </p><p>Area 2 detectives are investigating.<br></p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: A model to fix Illinois' broken primary system
entries.summary: <p>On Tuesday, a small number of people in Illinois will head to the polls to determine the party nominees for November’s general election. If history and current projections are any guide, we are looking at a turnout that would be considered a crisis in any other healthy democracy. Yet, in Illinois, we call it primary election day.</p><p>As millions of dollars flood our airwaves and mailboxes, the target isn't the average citizen or swing voter — it’s the "reliable" Democratic primary voter. Only <a class="Link" href="https://www.lwvil.org/news/illinois-election-update-may-2024" target="_blank" >19% of Illinois registered voters went to the polls </a>for the <a class="Link" href="https://www.cbsnews.com/chicago/primary-2024/" target="_blank" >2024 primary</a>. This trend holds nationally; only <a class="Link" href="https://bipartisanpolicy.org/press-release/voters-dont-participate-primaries/" target="_blank" >21.3% of eligible voters</a> participated in the 2022 primary. </p><p>Democratic candidates in Illinois and their consultants invest <a class="Link" href="https://www.fec.gov/data/candidates/senate/?election_year=2026&cycle=2026&election_full=true&is_active_candidate=true" target="_blank" >staggering sums of money</a> to target those reliable Democratic primary voters, knowing that whoever wins Tuesday in this “blue” state will easily win in November when the main election, in name only, takes place. Every candidate knows what the voters often miss: that the action is in March, not November.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-940000" name="module-940000"></a> <div class="RichTextSidebarModule-title">Commentary bug</div> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h2>Commentary</h2></div> </div><p>The current partisan primary system is a gatekeeper that <a class="Link" href="https://www.nytimes.com/2025/02/23/us/politics/us-elections-gerrymandering.html" target="_blank" >deprives voters of meaningful choices</a> in the general election. It incentivizes candidates to play to the furthest edges of their base and inevitably devolves into personality fights and negative campaigning, leaving the exhausted majority of voters to choose between two pre-selected options in November. </p><p>In 2024, <a class="Link" href="https://www.usnews.com/opinion/articles/2024-11-01/wait-has-the-election-already-been-decided" target="_blank" >effectively 87% of seats</a> in the House of Representatives were decided in the primary, and were “safe” in the general election, according to a report in U.S. News & World Report. For those of us in the thick of Democratic politics, this high-cost, low-participation churn has become the "new normal," but we should not accept this distortion of democracy as acceptable.</p><p>There is a better way: the Alaska model.</p><p>In 2020, Alaska moved to a nonpartisan "<a class="Link" href="https://journals.law.harvard.edu/jol/2024/11/05/the_alaska_model_for_democracy_in_elections/" target="_blank" >Top Four</a>" primary. There are no separate Democratic or Republican ballots. Every candidate — regardless of party — appears on a single ballot available to every voter. The top four finishers move on to the general election, where voters use <a class="Link" href="https://www.elections.alaska.gov/essentials/" target="_blank" >ranked choice voting</a> to ensure the winner has true majority support.</p><p>The results in Alaska have been a revelation:</p><ul class="rte2-style-ul" type="disc" style="margin-bottom: 0in; caret-color: rgb(0, 0, 0); color: rgb(0, 0, 0); font-style: normal; font-variant-caps: normal; font-weight: 400; letter-spacing: normal; orphans: 2; text-align: start; text-indent: 0px; text-transform: none; white-space: normal; widows: 2; word-spacing: 0px; -webkit-text-stroke-width: 0px; text-decoration-line: none; text-decoration-thickness: auto; text-decoration-style: solid;" id="rte-ce1ec8a7-2145-11f1-87c9-216eacd0e9d1"><li><b>Wider participation:</b> Voters aren't forced to join a party just to have a say. In their first cycle under this model, Alaska saw a massive increase in "meaningful votes"— ballots cast in truly competitive elections.  They elected <a class="Link" href="https://www.uniteamericainstitute.org/research/alaskas-election-model-how-the-top-four-nonpartisan-primary-system-improves-participation-competition-and-representation" target="_blank" >more women, created a bipartisan governing coalition and had the most productive legislative session</a> in recent memory, according to Unite America Institute.</li><li><b>Reduced polarization:</b> When you need to be one of the top four, and eventually need second-choice votes to win, you can’t afford to alienate half the electorate. Negative campaigning largely goes away, candidates appeal to voters on issues, and coalitions begin to form. Serious people are elevated; performative politicians cap out.</li><li><b>Appeal to the majority:</b> Right now, we spend an absurd amount of money to win a very targeted few votes because the system is designed for niche targeting among  reliable Democratic primary voters. A nonpartisan primary forces capital to compete for a broader, more representative audience, including the <a class="Link" href="https://independentvoterproject.org/voter-stats/il" target="_blank" >41% of Illinois voters</a> who identify as independents (versus <a class="Link" href="https://independentvoterproject.org/voter-stats/il" target="_blank" >39% Democrats</a>, <a class="Link" href="https://independentvoterproject.org/voter-stats/il" target="_blank" >20% Republicans</a>). Nationally, the number of voters identifying as independent is <a class="Link" href="https://iava.org/media/iava-surveyed-members-in-run-up-to-the-general-election-here-are-the-results" target="_blank" >higher among military veterans</a> (55%) and <a class="Link" href="https://news.gallup.com/poll/700499/new-high-identify-political-independents.aspx" target="_blank" >younger voters</a> (56%). </li></ul><p>We are currently sleepwalking through a process that degrades our democracy by exclusion. We’ve accepted a system where the "primary" is the only election that matters, yet it’s the one where the fewest people have a voice.</p><p>Illinois doesn't have to stay stuck in this cycle of expensive apathy. By adopting the Alaska model, we can move toward a system where every vote is a meaningful one, and where our representatives are accountable to the many, not the few.</p><p><i>Todd Connor is co-founder of Veterans for All Voters, a national grassroots organization advocating for political reforms to depolarize politics and expand democratic participation. He previously founded Bunker Labs and The Collective Academy, and he teaches social impact at the University of Chicago Booth School of Business.</i><br> </p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-920000" name="module-920000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody">Send letters to <a class="Link" href="mailto:letters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >letters@suntimes.com</a>. More about <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/submitting-opinion-and-letters" >how to submit here</a>.</div> </div><p></p>
entries.author: Todd Connor

entries.title: Extra-alarm blaze erupts at a Rogers Park building, seriously injuring firefighter
entries.summary: <p>A firefighter was at Stroger Hospital in serious condition after possibly falling through the roof of a Rogers Park apartment building that erupted in flames on Monday afternoon.</p><p>The 2-11 alarm fire was controlled about 12:15 p.m. at a multi-story building at 1757 W. North Shore Ave., according to fire spokesperson Larry Merritt. </p><p>"There was a mayday call and the firefighter was taken to Stroger in serious condition," Merritt said.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">2-11 in the 1700 block of West North Shore has been struck out. <a href="https://t.co/egTWqsQAF3">pic.twitter.com/egTWqsQAF3</a></p>— Chicago Fire Media (@CFDMedia) <a href="https://twitter.com/CFDMedia/status/2033593446569484554?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">March 16, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>A "mayday" is an emergency alert meaning they have lost communication with a firefighter who is inside the structure. </p><p>Over police radio, an OEMC dispatcher told an officer that the situation was a "mayday fire," and a firefighter fell through the roof. The firefighter arrived at Stroger at 12:28 p.m., the dispatcher said. </p><p>Several people including a dog had been displaced, according to the dispatcher. </p><p>As of 1 p.m. Chicago police officers had the scene blocked off to pedestrians and motorists.</p><p><i>This is a developing story. Check back for updates.</i></p>
entries.author: Kaitlin Washburn

entries.title: Fuerte tormenta primaveral golpea Chicago con vientos de más de 50 millas por hora y nieve
entries.summary: <p>La zona de Chicago experimentó tormentas severas y vientos el domingo, que se transformaron en nieve y condiciones de tormenta de nieve durante la noche.</p><p>El Servicio Meteorológico Nacional (NWS) advirtió que una "fuerte tormenta primaveral" se estaba acercando a la región el domingo y permanecería hasta el lunes. El servicio meteorológico emitió un aviso por vientos fuertes del sur que podrían alcanzar entre 50 y 65 millas por hora desde la tarde del domingo hasta la noche.</p><p>Se pronosticaban chubascos y tormentas eléctricas, potencialmente severas, en el área de Chicago la noche del domingo. Después de las 6 p.m., el NWS emitió un aviso de inundación para el Condado de Cook hasta las 9:30 p.m. Se emitieron alertas de tormenta eléctrica severa para partes del centro norte de Illinois, mientras que se emitió una alerta de tornado para partes de Gibson City y Elliot.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-980000" name="module-980000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>Se esperaba que la lluvia se convirtiera de aguanieve a nieve durante la noche, con ráfagas de viento de hasta 45 millas por hora, según el servicio meteorológico. Se pronosticaron de una a tres pulgadas de nieve, dijo el servicio meteorológico.</p><p>Las áreas al noroeste de la autopista I-55 vieron nieve golpeada por el viento durante la noche del domingo hasta el lunes, lo que pudo dar lugar a condiciones de tormenta de nieve en ocasiones. Esto llevó a condiciones de viaje peligrosas a nivel local, según el servicio meteorológico.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://x.com/NWSChicago/status/2033266084992651585?s=20" target="_blank" >https://x.com/NWSChicago/status/2033266084992651585?s=20</a></p><p>Los vuelos en el Aeropuerto Internacional O'Hare estaban experimentando demoras durante todo el día. El vuelo promedio que llegaba tenía más de 80 minutos de retraso, y los vuelos de salida estaban retrasados casi 40 minutos. En el Aeropuerto Internacional Midway, las demoras eran de aproximadamente 20 minutos. Para las 7 p.m. del domingo, 1,005 vuelos habían sido cancelados en las últimas 24 horas en O'Hare, y 111 vuelos fueron cancelados en Midway durante ese mismo período.</p><p>Un vuelo de SkyWest con destino a Portland, Maine, regresó a O'Hare poco después del despegue debido a un reporte de un rayo. El vuelo, que operaba como United Express, aterrizó de manera segura, y "estamos trabajando para ayudar a los clientes a continuar con sus viajes lo más rápido posible", dijo la aerolínea.</p><p>La lluvia empapó a los asistentes del desfile irlandés del lado sur antes de que comenzaran las festividades el domingo por la tarde, pero eso no detuvo a aquellos vestidos de verde, ansiosos por la música de gaitas y los dulces gratis.</p><p>Las nubes eventualmente dieron paso a la luz del sol, que duró la mayor parte del desfile, antes de que regresara la lluvia con ráfagas de viento. Algunos se retiraron mientras otros se cubrían con ponchos mientras el desfile continuaba.</p><p>Los cielos probablemente se despejarán para el martes, pero las temperaturas permanecerán en los 20 grados. El servicio meteorológico espera que las temperaturas aumenten a niveles de 40 y 50 grados hacia el final de la semana.</p><p>Contribuyeron: Kaitlin Washburn, Violet Miller, Elleiana Green</p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Every known federal prosecution in Chicago tied to Trump’s immigration blitz
entries.summary: <p>Federal prosecutors in Chicago have accused 32 known defendants of nonimmigration crimes tied to Operation Midway Blitz. Many were accused of assaulting or resisting federal agents or officers.</p><p>Twenty of those defendants have already been cleared. Grand jurors refused to indict at least three of the 20, and another member of that group was found not guilty at trial.</p><p>Meanwhile, prosecutors have entered into deferred-prosecution agreements with two additional defendants. In each of those cases, if that person abides by certain terms over a set period of time, the feds have agreed to drop the charges in that case as well.</p><p>The Chicago Sun-Times is tracking all of the known criminal cases tied to Operation Midway Blitz:</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-6e0000" name="html-embed-module-6e0000"></a><div class="flourish-embed flourish-table" data-src="visualisation/27349648"><script src="https://public.flourish.studio/resources/embed.js"></script><noscript><img src="https://public.flourish.studio/visualisation/27349648/thumbnail" width="100%" alt="table visualization" /></noscript></div> </div> </div><div class="PageListO" data-module data-show-loadmore="true" ><a class="AnchorLink" id="list-bb0000" name="list-bb0000"></a> <div class="PageList-header" > <div class="PageList-header-title-wrap" > <div class="PageList-header-title"> Latest from Jon Seidel </div> </div> </div> <div class="PageListO-items"> <div class="PageListO-items-row"> <div class="PageListO-items-column" data-with-borders> <div class="PageListO-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2026/03/13/coalicion-dice-que-fiscal-del-condado-de-cook-eileen-oneill-burke-ignoro-la-violencia-de-ice" >Coalición dice que fiscal del condado ignoró la violencia de agentes federales; buscan quien responsabilice a ICE</a> </div> <div class="PageListO-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/03/13/chicago-trump-abughazaleh-justice-department-broadview-midway-blitz-ice" >Remaining Broadview 6 defendants want records showing Trump administration's influence on conspiracy case</a> </div> <div class="PageListO-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2026/03/13/fiscalia-retira-los-cargos-de-conspiracion-contra-2-manifestantes-de-ice" >Fiscalía retira los cargos de conspiración contra 2 manifestantes de ICE</a> </div> <div class="PageListO-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/03/12/chicago-midway-blitz-immigration-trump-kat-abughazaleh-sharp-joselyn-walsh-broadview-six" >Broadview 4? Feds drop conspiracy charges against 2 ICE protesters, including Dem who ended campaign</a> </div> <div class="PageListO-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/03/12/chicago-cook-county-oneill-burke-trump-ice-border-patrol-midway-blitz-special-prosecutor" >Seeking special prosecutor to target ICE, coalition says O'Neill Burke turned 'blind eye' to feds' violence</a> </div> <div class="PageListO-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2026/03/11/a-pesar-de-las-advertencias-no-hay-senales-de-aumento-de-la-presencia-de-ice-esta-primavera" >A pesar de las advertencias pasadas, no hay señales de aumento de la presencia de ICE esta primavera</a> </div> <div class="PageListO-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2026/03/10/chicago-immigration-deport-trump-noem-bovino-midway-blitz" >No signs of an ICE surge in Chicago this spring, despite past warnings</a> </div> <div class="PageListO-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2026/03/02/juez-ordena-liberar-a-32-detenidos-del-centro-de-detencion-de-broadview-pero-algunos-ya-han-sido-deportados" >Juez ordena liberar a 32 detenidos del centro de detención de Broadview, pero algunos ya han sido deportados</a> </div> </div> </div> </div> </div><p></p>
entries.author: Jon Seidel

entries.title: Jesse Jackson family disputes posthumous Juliana Stratton endorsement in Senate primary
entries.summary: <p>The family of <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/politics/2026/03/06/rev-jesse-jackson-chicago-service-house-of-hope-pullman-neighborhood-barack-obama-jb-pritzker" >Rev. Jesse Jackson</a> is withdrawing an eyebrow-raising posthumous announcement of an endorsement of Lt. Gov. Juliana Stratton for U.S. Senate, saying on Monday that his primary choices were not finalized before his death last month.</p><p>Stratton's campaign on Saturday released a brief statement that said the Jackson family had announced "he had personally endorsed" Stratton, with Jackson making his final endorsements "before his passing in February." It also said Yusef Jackson, one of Jackson's sons, also endorsed Stratton.</p><p>The Stratton statement also featured a picture of Stratton with Jackson. The campaign quoted Pastor Stephen J. Thurston, a close family friend of the Jacksons, saying the civil rights leader saw in Stratton an "unrelenting commitment to the people of Illinois." </p><p>But on Monday morning, Yusef Jackson took back the endorsement, writing that while his father had begun reviewing primary candidates, as he has done in previous cycles, he never finalized his picks before his death. </p><p>"Out of respect for my father, we decided not to publicly release his intended selections given the process had not been finalized," Yusef Jackson said. "Unfortunately, this weekend, a draft sample ballot that was in development was released without authorization. It is important to note that the Jackson Family does not issue political endorsements, nor do Rainbow PUSH or the Rainbow PUSH Coalition."</p><p>Both Stratton and Senate contender Rep. Robin Kelly are also using past endorsements from former President Barack Obama in ads in the Senate race, raising more eyebrows — and showing the race may be tightening. The other frontrunner in Tuesday's Democratic primary to replace retiring longtime Sen. Dick Durbin is U.S. Rep. Raja Krishnamoorthi from Schaumburg.</p><p>Also on the Democratic primary ballot are: Steve Botsford Jr., Sean Brown, Awisi A. Bustos, Jonathan Dean, Bryan Maxwell, Kevin Ryan and Christopher Swann. In the Republican primary, candidates include R. Cary Capparelli, Casey Chlebek, Jeannie Evans, Pamela Denise Long, Jimmie Lee Tillman II and Don Tracy. </p>
entries.author: Tina Sfondeles

entries.title: Five ways Marist turned into a postseason juggernaut
entries.summary: <p>Once Marist took care of business in the lead up and arrived in Champaign with its very talented roster and 31 wins, the idea of capturing the program’s first-ever state championship became very real. When the RedHawks ultimately did, it wasn’t a stunner.</p><p>But what no one saw coming was Marist’s domination in doing so.</p><p>Although I believed Marist was, from top to bottom, one of the three most talented teams all season — and picked them to reach Champaign when state tournament play began — what developed in Champaign was not in the forecast.</p><p>By that I mean the control, command and ease the RedHawks had in dismantling York and then beating the defending state champs Benet, a team it had lost to in late February, in the state championship.</p><p>There were some obvious strengths and positive developments when it came to Marist as the season played out. Getting healthy mattered, mostly from a depth and competitive standpoint, and the impact of Charles Barnes, an All-Area performer, was clear to see.</p><p>But here are five ways and reasons Marist turned itself into a postseason juggernaut.</p><p><b>1. Marist’s athletic attributes led to a different level of defense.</b></p><p>The size, length, strength and athleticism of the RedHawks really stood out and became difference-making.</p><p>Those aforementioned strengths raised the level of its defense, becoming Marist’s identity. When teams guard at this level, they are never too far from a championship, and it’s why this RedHawks team looked far different from past versions.</p><p>As a result, coach Brian Hynes’ group took over and suffocated both York and Benet. The RedHawks played with purpose defensively while their physical attributes overwhelmed foes at times.</p><p>Whether trying to speed up York or settling in to stop Benet’s balance and versatility on offense, the scoring opportunities became scarce against the Marist defense. Hynes' gameplan also impressed and was followed and executed.</p><p>It’s why York shot 14 of 42 from the field (33 percent) and Benet a paltry 28 percent. The Redwings made 11 shots the entire game. This is a Benet team that scored 60 or more points 23 times this season.</p><p><b>2. Marist’s schedule was better than people thought.</b></p><p>Marist had a terrific season a year ago. The RedHawks won a school record 31 games and reached a sectional final. But if there was a knock on last year’s RedHawks, it was that the schedule lacked marquee non-conference matchups in preparation for the state tournament.</p><p>This season’s schedule was a step up, despite continued questions about quality wins and schedule strength.</p><p>Marist played two good out-of-state opponents in Sidwell Friends from Washington, DC, and Webster Groves from Missouri, traditionally strong programs that both won 20-plus games this season.</p><p>There were losses to very good teams: three-time state champ DePaul and a pair of ranked conference foes in defending 4A champ Benet and St. Patrick.</p><p>The RedHawks beat a talented and ranked Evanston at the Centralia Holiday Tournament. They then took down Centralia, something that’s never easy to do in Centralia’s gym. It was a Centralia team that finished 32-2 on the season.</p><p>There was a win over ranked Waukegan, and they throttled Hyde Park, a 3A supersectional team, by 28 points.</p><p>This veteran team received — and passed — more tests than given credit for during the season.</p><p><b>3. The regional championship win over Rich meant a lot — and should have been a warning for all.</b></p><p>The regional championship matchup with Rich was billed and viewed as one of the top regional title games in the state.</p><p>Rich, even as a No. 9 seed, was playing its best basketball of the season. The Raptors returned its best player in recent weeks, played the game on its home floor and knocked out Marist the year before. Coach Lou Adams’ team was the ultimate dangerous low seed. There were upset vibes floating in the air in the south suburbs on regional championship night.</p><p>The RedHawks made it a laugher, crushing Rich 82-54. Although Marist was supposed to win, the way it did should have opened eyes and led you to believe that maybe this team was on a mission and could reach its ceiling.</p><p>What that win over Rich certainly did was provide a sigh of relief and a massive confidence boost for this team.</p><p>With the state title run now in the rearview mirror, coach Brian Hynes’ concern with that particular game can be detailed. In talking with Hynes after that Rich win, there was a definite concern and worry — even as the No. 2 sectional seed — heading into that matchup.</p><p>He called it a “relief” to have that win over Rich in the regional championship done and dusted.</p><p>That dominant play continued and was prevalent throughout the postseason. Remember, the RedHawks encountered just one tight game in their entire state tournament run — a 56-53 win over Peoria Richwoods in the super.</p><p><b>4. Stephen Brown’s impact and role.</b></p><p>The career of Stephen Brown was an interesting one since the day he entered high school as a much-talked-about freshman.</p><p>There were some who ranked Brown as the top-ranked freshman in the class, so there were huge expectations placed on the kid from the jump. He had a productive freshman season as he tried to shed a big man/5-man label and become more perimeter oriented to enhance his college prospects.</p><p>While there was always promise and upside as he progressed through the years, splitting time with football and injuries certainly slowed down his basketball development. Ultimately, he chose football and signed with North Carolina State.</p><p>Then it was about finding his way as an impactful presence for this particular 2025-2026 team. He no longer needed to live up to any individual expectations on the basketball floor.</p><p>Although he put up nice numbers in the state tournament run, better and more impactful than at any time during his senior year, it was more than just statistics when it came to Brown’s influence on this team. He settled in and played to his strengths.</p><p>Brown’s engagement, activity level and presence, combined with his tremendous athletic qualities, had a profound impact down the stretch.</p><p><b>5. Marist was always better than people thought.</b></p><p>Marist began the season ranked No. 4 in the Sun-Times Super 25. That’s an indication of the talent and depth this team possessed.</p><p>And then somehow along the way the RedHawks fell out of favor during the regular season, both in perception and in the rankings.</p><p>There always appeared to be either questions or doubt when it came to Marist.</p><p>But the RedHawks really did nothing to disappoint for months. They didn’t lose to an in-state team until Jan. 24.</p><p>They were out of sight, out of mind during the holidays while making their annual trek to the southern part of the state, but that two-month run to start the season included an impressive Centralia Holiday Tournament title run over ranked teams.</p><p>Late January losses to DePaul Prep and St. Patrick pushed Marist outside the top 10. They didn’t return for over a month as the RedHawks entered postseason play ranked No. 11.</p><p>When watching Marist closely — very closely — and all it brought to the table with veteran guards, the size and length, versatility, improved defense and a bonafide difference-maker in Charles Barnes, it should not have been a huge surprise that the RedHawks would be playing in Champaign.</p><p>And then once you’re there? Never underestimate the confidence that comes and the transformation that can occur by winning five straight high-pressure, win-or-go-home, state tournament games.</p><p>When you watch a team you sometimes need to look at its very best and how that best stacks up against the rest. When it came to Marist, its best was good enough.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-670000" name="html-embed-module-670000"></a><iframe title="No Shot Clock, Ep. 206: Breaking down the 2026 IHSA state finals" allowtransparency="true" height="150" width="100%" style="border: none; min-width: min(100%, 430px);height:150px;" scrolling="no" data-name="pb-iframe-player" src="https://www.podbean.com/player-v2/?i=iz5re-1a6f511-pb&from=pb6admin&share=1&download=1&rtl=0&fonts=Arial&skin=1&font-color=auto&logo_link=episode_page&btn-skin=c73a3a" loading="lazy"></iframe> </div> </div><p></p>
entries.author: Joe Henricksen

entries.title: Recapping the 3A and 4A IHSA basketball state finals
entries.summary: <p>As Marist captured its first-ever state championship in Class 4A, and East St. Louis won a title it was expected to win in Class 3A, the weekend closed the book on three other teams that fell short but have done a whole lot of winning in recent years.</p><p>DePaul Prep’s heartbreaking, last-second defeat to Benet in the semifinals prevented the Rams a shot at playing for a fourth straight state championship. But coach Tom Kleinschmidt and his Rams added to an expanding trophy case.</p><p>That’s five straight years of taking home a trophy, including state championships in 2023, 2024 and 2025. The Rams’ third-place finish this season matched the third–place finish from 2022.</p><p>DePaul’s record over that five-year run is 149-28.</p><p>Benet, which was looking to win back-to-back state championships, fell to Marist in the 4A title game. But what a four years it’s been for the program.</p><p>Coach Gene Heidkamp’s Redwings have averaged 32 wins a year over the past four seasons, going 131-16 with a state championship in 2025 and two state runner-up finishes in 2023 and 2026.</p><p>With 99 victories, no team has won more games over the past three seasons in Illinois than DePaul Prep.</p><p>Benet is next with 96. No surprise.</p><p>The team with the third most wins, however, might surprise you: Kaneland.</p><p>With their win in the Class 3A third-place game, the Knights notched their 94th win over the past three seasons.</p><p>After not winning a sectional championship in over four decades, Kaneland has won back-to-back sectional titles and brought home the program’s first state trophy since the 1972-73 season.</p><p>➤ Time to pay homage to Jake Pollack.</p><p>Pollack was an All-Area selection and is a legitimate Division I prospect headed to the Ivy League. But the Dartmouth recruit didn’t have the biggest name in the Class of 2026.</p><p>Nonetheless, with a Deerfield postseason tournament run that culminated with a state runner-up finish in Class 3A, the 6-8 senior certainly left his mark.</p><p>Pollack put up solid numbers throughout his career and senior year, averaging 17.7 points and 7.6 rebounds a game this season. He responded all season to whatever an opposing team threw his way, which was usually a double-team.</p><p>The production and the poise he played with continued in Champaign this past weekend as he led the Warriors.</p><p>With 20 points, seven rebounds, four assists and five blocks, Pollack did a little of everything in the state semifinal overtime win over Kaneland. That included impacting beyond the stat sheet, and when it comes to those variables, what Pollack does it hard to define but easy to see.</p><p>When the ball needed to be brought up the court against Kaneland pressure, it was the big Deerfield big man who did it. When the team needed a play to stop the opposing team’s momentum, it was Pollack who provided it. And his effervescent play was constantly on display with his calming presence and leadership for the Warriors.</p><p>➤ Watching Marist attack York and Benet in such a different way in its two wins in Champaign, showcased the pulse coach Brian Hynes had on his team.</p><p>It’s never easy with a quick 24-hour turnaround to make adjustments, but Hynes’ gameplan clearly hit home with a locked-in group of players. The RedHawks’ execution was outstanding.</p><p>➤ Watch out for Leo.</p><p>Although Leo wasn’t a huge surprise — the Lions were a No. 1 sectional seed — coach Jimalle Ridley did a terrific job in guiding a young team to the IHSA State Finals.</p><p>While Leo dropped two games in Champaign to finish fourth in the state in Class 3A, the Lions could very well be back.</p><p>Led by junior guard Brian Kizer, Leo will return all five starters. Plus, the development of the three young players that contributed off the bench, including promising 6-8 freshman big man Elon Henderson, will only raise the level of this team.</p><p>This is a program now with a solid foundation and a team that should have state title aspirations next season.</p><p>➤ No one could have anticipated Benet’s offense stalling in Champaign.</p><p>Benet, which scored 60 or more points 23 times, averaged 64 points a game this season with starters sitting out many fourth quarters due to lopsided wins.</p><p>In the two games played in Champaign, the Redwings combined to score 67 points. They didn’t score in the second quarter and scored just three points in the fourth quarter of the state championship game.</p><p>The 28 points Benet scored in the championship game is the lowest point total by a Gene Heidkamp team in 12 years when Benet lost to Simeon 46-26 in the 2014 Pontiac Holiday Tournament.</p><p>➤ Benet’s title game clunker surely stings. But the Redwings played the final game on the final night of the season in back-to-back years. That’s what you play for and that’s what this group should and will remember years from now.</p><p>Seniors Jayden Wright and Colin Stack wrap up their careers with a state title, second-place finish and a combined 69 wins in two years. That’s some stellar work.</p><p>➤ The cleanest, most entertaining 3A/4A game of the State Finals weekend was the first one: Deerfield’s 49-48 win over Kaneland on Friday morning.</p><p>The game featured a little of everything, including a double-digit comeback, several lead changes down the stretch and the drama of an overtime.</p><p>There were made shots as Deerfield shot 47 percent from beyond the arc (8 of 17) and the two teams combined for 15 threes in the game.</p><p>And there were big shots.</p><p>Deerfield’s Tommy Donahue drained a three to put the Warriors up 42-40 with two minutes to play. Kaneland’s Evan Frieders answered with a three-pointer of his own 17 seconds later, giving the Knights a 43-42 lead. And Marshawn Cocroft’s running lay-up with 11 seconds left to sent the game to overtime.</p><p>➤ York’s dream season ended with a bit of a thud in Champaign. But is there a coach who maximized more with what he had than Mike Dunn?</p><p>➤ Something still feels off about the IHSA State Finals format when there are third-place games being played on Friday night rather than state semifinal showdowns. It certainly hurts the television audience for those around the state who would like to tune in after work on Friday night.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-670000" name="html-embed-module-670000"></a><iframe title="No Shot Clock, Ep. 206: Breaking down the 2026 IHSA state finals" allowtransparency="true" height="150" width="100%" style="border: none; min-width: min(100%, 430px);height:150px;" scrolling="no" data-name="pb-iframe-player" src="https://www.podbean.com/player-v2/?i=iz5re-1a6f511-pb&from=pb6admin&share=1&download=1&rtl=0&fonts=Arial&skin=1&font-color=auto&logo_link=episode_page&btn-skin=c73a3a" loading="lazy"></iframe> </div> </div><p></p>
entries.author: Joe Henricksen

entries.title: Crypto, AI groups spend millions in Chicago primaries
entries.summary: <h3>Good morning, Chicago. ✶</h3><p>🔎 <b>Below:</b> More than 60 L riders have been recorded getting pushed onto the tracks since 2021, a Sun-Times analysis found.</p><p>🗞️ <b>Plus:</b> AI and cryptocurrency PACS spend millions on Chicago area primaries, how Black-owned breweries are surviving industry setbacks and more news you need to know.</p><p>📝 <b>Keeping href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2026/03/16/chicago-transit-authority-shoving-incidents-train-platforms-trump-administration-targeting-crime" >An L rider gets shoved onto tracks from CTA platforms every month, on average</a></h3><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/authors/robert-herguth" target="_blank" >Robert Herguth</a> and <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/authors/david-struett" target="_blank" >David Struett</a></h5><p><b>By the numbers:</b> More than 60 instances of an L rider being pushed onto train tracks have been recorded in CTA paperwork since 2021, a Sun-Times analysis found. That's an average of one incident a month. Records don’t show anybody dying from being pushed during that time frame, but there were injuries and lots of close calls — and in past years, there have been fatalities.</p><p><b>The motives:</b> Most of the incidents appeared intentional, sometimes involving people with apparent mental health issues, and other times the outcome of another crime such as a stick-up or a fight, records show.</p><p><b>Key context:</b> For decades, the CTA has faced criticism about crime. The agency has emphasized the system is relatively safe given how many riders it has. But leaders have at times seemed to prioritize cost-savings over safety, while increasingly relying on security technology that may help catch offenders but can’t intervene when a rider is being mugged or beaten.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2026/03/16/chicago-transit-authority-shoving-incidents-train-platforms-trump-administration-targeting-crime"
