</figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2026/03/13/cryptocurrency-ai-join-in-31-million-super-pac-blitz-in-four-congressional-primaries" >Cryptocurrency, AI join $31 million super PAC blitz in four congressional primaries</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/mawa-iqbal-wbez" target="_blank" >Mawa Iqbal</a>, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chip-mitchell-wbez" target="_blank" >Chip Mitchell</a> and <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/authors/mariah-woelfel" target="_blank" >Mariah Woelfel</a></h5><p><b>Crypto, AI influence:</b> National special interest groups, including those for deep-pocketed cryptocurrency and artificial intelligence, have spent tens of millions of dollars to influence four hotly contested Democratic congressional primaries in the Chicago area.</p><p><b>Millions from PACs:</b> As of Thursday, super PACs reported spending more than $31.4 million, including more than $6.1 million for attack ads ahead of Tuesday’s primaries. Crypto, AI and pro-Israel groups spent a total $26.9 million, a WBEZ review of federal campaign disclosures found.</p><p><b>Rules on spending:</b> Super PACs cannot donate directly to candidates or coordinate with them but can spend unlimited sums on their behalf.</p><p><b>Key context:</b> There's far more super PAC spending since the last time the Chicago area had U.S. House primaries without an incumbent. In 2022, U.S. Rep. Jonathan Jackson’s campaign saw more than $1.1 million in such support, while U.S. Rep. Delia Ramirez’s got a $1.5 million boost. This election cycle, a handful of congressional candidates are seeing quadruple those amounts go toward their individual runs.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2026/03/13/cryptocurrency-ai-join-in-31-million-super-pac-blitz-in-four-congressional-primaries" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h4>IMMIGRATION ✶</h4><div focal point of campaigns. Tuesday’s primary elections will serve as a test of whether campaign messaging about fighting ICE worked — and how views on immigration have changed during Trump’s second term.</p><p><b>Views on ICE:</b> In Chicago, 75% of those polled overall had an unfavorable view on ICE. creative</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/authors/erica-thompson" target="_blank" >Erica Thompson</a></h5><p><b>Cultural focus:</b> Leaning into cultural celebration is one way Moor’s Brewing and Funkytown Brewery — the city’s two Black-owned breweries — set themselves apart in a volatile industry that has seen increased brewery closings nationwide. In Chicago, at least five have shuttered or announced closures this year amid rising costs and a decline in the U.S. drinking rate.</p><p><b>Taking it slow:</b> Moor’s and Funkytown have remained resilient by producing their beers at other breweries instead of opening brick-and-mortar locations right away. In most cases, contract brewing is not only a strategy but also a necessity, as Black-owned businesses struggle to gain access to capital, industry connections, distribution and marketing opportunities, according to the national nonprofit Brewers Association.</p><p><b>Creating community:</b> These breweries have built a following by pairing beer with cultural experiences and events and by targeting a diverse consumer base. "It was never just purely about the beer itself," said Rich Bloomfield, founder of Funkytown Brewery. "It’s about community and creating a sense of belonging. We believe that we have a new, contemporary voice... that speaks to underserved demographics and Gen Z. It combines community, art, music, sport and culture together."</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/food-drink/2026/03/15/black-owned-breweries-chicago-craft-beer-industry" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/arts-and-culture/2026/03/15/south-side-irish-parade-tradition-st-patricks-day" >South Side Irish Parade brings pride, old and new</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-i-wtyzuk-l-tl/" target="_blank" >Violet Miller</a></h5><p>On Sunday, 47 years after Morgan Park residents George Hendry and Pat Coakley started their own South Side Irish Parade, a crowd once again watched as bagpipers and Irish dancers marched by.</p><p>As it has since 1981, the parade stepped off from 103rd Street down Western Avenue to 115th Street — despite intermittent sheets of rain and gusts of wind.</p><p>Tom Wognum, who brought his 8-year-old son Milo for the eighth time, said nothing would deter the duo.</p><p>"We have been out here in the snow, we have been out here in shorts and T-shirts," Wognum said. "It’s Chicago in March, there’s no way to predict any of it. But whatever the weather, we’re going to come out."</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/arts-and-culture/2026/03/15/south-side-irish-parade-tradition-st-patricks-day" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h4>YOUR DAILY QUESTION ☕️</h4><p>Yesterday, we asked you: <u>Do you have a favorite local craft beer? Tell us about it.</u></p><p>Here’s some of what you said…</p><p>“Dovetail’s Rauchbier is delightfully smoky." — <i>Michael Sewall</i></p><p>“The Half Acre Daisy Cutter was the one beer I drank and then asked myself, 'Why drink the macro beer stuff?' It was just so good." — <i>John Mueller</i></p><p>“Werk Force Brewing ... beers are delicious and the brewery next to the train tracks is killer! Great vibes!" — <i>Katie Kriz DeRoss</i></p><hr><p>Thanks for reading the Sun-Times Morning Edition!<br>Got a story you think we missed? <a class="Link" href="mailto:newsletters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >Email us here.</a></p><hr><p><b>Written and curated by</b>: Phyllis Cha<br><b>Editor</b>: Eydie Cubarrubia</p><hr><p><i>The Chicago Sun-Times is a nonprofit supported by readers like you. Become a member to make stories like these free and available to everyone. Learn more at </i><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjhkill-l-th/" target="_blank" ><i>suntimes.com/member</i></a><i>.</i></p>

entries.title: Mayor Johnson signals support for letting CPS students take a day off to push for school funding
entries.summary: <p>Mayor Brandon Johnson on Monday hinted strongly that he supports the Chicago Teachers Union’s call to officially sanction a one-day strike on May 1 that would allow students at Chicago Public Schools to miss a day of classes to make their case for increased school funding.</p><p>Johnson is a former middle school teacher and paid organizer for the CTU — whose mayoral campaign was buoyed by $5.6 million in campaign contributions and hundreds of foot soldiers provided by the CTU and its affiliates.</p><p>His decision to declare a record $1 billion tax increment financing surplus helped to rescue CPS and bankroll the new teachers contract.</p><p>Now the teachers union wants the mayor and CPS's hybrid school board still narrowly controlled by Johnson’s appointees to essentially sanction May 1 as a school holiday, which would allow students to participate in a “no school, no work, no shopping” day.</p><p>Appearing Monday on WBEZ-FM Radio’s “Ask the Mayor” program, Johnson said his longstanding push to tax major corporations and the wealthy Chicagoans to bolster school funding has so far gone nowhere — and it’s time to push the envelope by turning talk into protest action.</p><p>"People all over the country are tired. Working people have had to bear the weight of a sluggish economy while the ultra-rich continue to get richer. Our schools are woefully underfunded… People want more investment in our public transportation system,” the mayor said. “All of that requires a push from working people to challenge elected leaders.”</p><p>WBEZ host Sasha-Ann Simons noted that CPS students already will be off Tuesday and Wednesday this week, and will be off all next week for spring break and again on April 3.</p><p>Johnson said he's aware of that, but argued that CPS parents also "want their schools to be fully-funded." </p><p>“If we just believe that we can just sit on our hands and just hope and wish, that clearly has not worked. I’ve called for taxing corporations and I’ve had other elected leaders fight me who are also Democrats," he said.</p><p>Johnson said Rev. Jesse Jackson, the civil rights icon who died last month at the age of 84, would never have achieved all that he did without “direct action and organizing.” He “literally had to protest to ensure that we have a right to vote,” a right that is now under threat, the mayor said.</p><p>“We are experiencing unprecedented levels of attack [on] the gains that our ancestors made. And if we believe for one minute that the way in which we’re gonna receive our God-given rights our is by just simply asking questions or having town halls — absent taking it to the streets — we are missing the moment,” the mayor said. “We have to push… government to do right by working people. And that will require protest.”</p><p>Ald. Jeanette Taylor (20th), Johnson’s handpicked chair of the City Council’s Education Committee, has two grandchildren who attend Chicago Public Schools. Taylor said she does not believe the mayor should sanction students to skip classes, unless they learn something from it.</p><p>“Are they going down to Springfield? Are they coming down to City Hall? What work is gonna be done? There needs to be something else other than just taking a day off. I want young people to understand them taking the day off. Are we teaching them about May Day? Are we teaching them about workers rights? Are we teaching them to advocate in Springfield for themselves? What comes with it?” Taylor said.</p><p>"It has to be a day of action. Don’t ask me to let my kids take a day off of school so they can sit at home, because we know what they’re gonna do at home. They’re gonna be on the internet. They’re gonna be doing everything but what they’re supposed to be doing.”</p><p>The resolution approved by the CTU’s 730-member House of Delegates last week calls for CPS to join a national movement embracing <a class="Link" href="https://maydaystrong.org/" target="_blank" >a day of “no school, no work, no shopping</a>.” Participants plan to call for higher taxes on the wealthy, better-funded schools, protections for immigrants and other reforms.</p><p>Union leaders cited several reasons for participating, including to demand that ICE gets “out of our cities,” referring to the U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement agency, and to push to “tax the rich to support our schools and vital services,” according to the resolution. It also says public education is facing attacks from “MAGA politicians” who support the policies of President Donald Trump and “corporate interests."</p><p>The union wants members and students to use the day to take part in “age-appropriate” civic education, labor history programming, voter registration and know-your-rights training, as well as rallies and marches.</p>
entries.author: Fran Spielman

entries.title: Remaining ‘Broadview Six’ defendants want conspiracy charge tossed, argue protesting isn’t a crime
entries.summary: <p>Arguing that federal prosecutors have made a “crime out of what under the Constitution cannot be a crime,” the group of protesters<b> </b>originally known as the "Broadview Six" asked a judge Monday to toss the conspiracy charge<b> </b>filed against them because it runs afoul of the First Amendment’s freedom of assembly.</p><p>It’s the latest attempt to challenge the indictment brought against the group<b> </b>— and one that a defendant in the case had an active role in drafting. The prosecution is one of several to result from the Trump administration's Operation Midway Blitz deportation campaign.</p><p>The group is now down to four, after prosecutors<b> </b><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/03/12/chicago-midway-blitz-immigration-trump-kat-abughazaleh-sharp-joselyn-walsh-broadview-six" target="_blank" >dropped<b> </b>the charges against<b> </b>two</a> of the defendants last week. Those who remain say the government’s allegations of conspiracy — a felony that could lead to a prison sentence of up to six years — enhances a misdemeanor charge based on the defendants’ “exercise of their First Amendment rights of assembly and association.”</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/03/12/chicago-midway-blitz-immigration-trump-kat-abughazaleh-sharp-joselyn-walsh-broadview-six" >Broadview 4? Feds drop conspiracy charges against 2 ICE protesters, including Dem who ended campaign</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-hangRight> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Brian Straw</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Village of Oak Park</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>“That a misdemeanor … can be escalated to a felony conspiracy charge simply because it occurred in the midst of an otherwise peaceable assembly creates a substantial risk of chilling the public’s willingness to exercise their First Amendment rights of freedom of assembly and freedom of speech,” defense attorneys wrote in an 18-page motion<b> </b>filed Monday.</p><p>The motion was filed on behalf of all the remaining defendants. But one of them, Oak Park village trustee and lawyer Brian Straw, played an active role in drafting it. That's according to Straw attorney Christopher Parente, who said Straw invested "a lot of his blood, sweat and tears."</p><p>"I think the bigger issue for Brian is the right for people to peacefully assemble and to protest their government, regardless of what the issue is," Parente told the Chicago Sun-Times.</p><p>Charged along with Straw are 45th Ward Democratic committeeperson Michael Rabbitt, congressional candidate Kat<b> </b>Abughazaleh,<b> </b>and a senior member of Abughazaleh's campaign staff, Andre Martin. Abughazaleh is <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/elections/2026/02/25/congressional-9th-district-15-democrats-march-17-primary-jan-schakowsky-seat" target="_blank" >seeking the Democratic nomination</a><b> </b>in Tuesday’s primary for Illinois’ 9th District<b> </b>congressional seat.</p><p>Prosecutors last week dropped charges against Joselyn<b> </b>Walsh, a musician, and Catherine “Cat” Sharp, a Democrat who dropped her bid for Cook County Board to focus on her legal defense. The feds also recently narrowed<b> </b>their theory of the case.</p><p>Challenges have followed in recent days from the four defendants who still face trial May 26. They sought records Friday<b> </b>that <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/03/13/chicago-trump-abughazaleh-justice-department-broadview-midway-blitz-ice" target="_blank" >might show an improper influence</a> on the case<b> </b>by the Trump administration.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/03/13/chicago-trump-abughazaleh-justice-department-broadview-midway-blitz-ice" >Remaining Broadview 6 defendants want records showing Trump administration’s influence on conspiracy case</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>Now they’ve asked U.S. District Judge April Perry<b> </b>to toss the conspiracy charge on First Amendment grounds, arguing that the feds' theory "puts any protester at risk of being charged with a felony on account of being in the wrong place at the wrong time with others." The lawyers are due back in Perry’s courtroom March 31. Each of the four also face a misdemeanor charge alleging they impeded an officer.</p><p>The conspiracy charge revolves around events on the morning of Sept. 26 outside the U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement facility<b> </b>in Broadview. It became a hotbed for protests during Midway Blitz.</p><p>The indictment alleges that, while a federal agent drove a vehicle<b> </b>toward the ICE facility, the defendants and others surrounded it. Members of the larger crowd allegedly<b> </b>banged on the vehicle, pushed against it, scratched it and even etched the word “PIG”<b> </b>into it.</p><p>The crowd allegedly broke a side mirror<b> </b>and a rear windshield wiper<b> </b>and forced the agent “to drive at an extremely slow rate of speed.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> alt="ABUGHAZALEHARRAIGNED-111325-111225_006.jpg" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/39e874a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7178x4028+0+380/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F08%2Fef%2Fc441d8eb46cfb97cee0dfa649c79%2Fabughazaleharraigned-111325-111225-006.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f259dc8/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7178x4028+0+380/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F08%2Fef%2Fc441d8eb46cfb97cee0dfa649c79%2Fabughazaleharraigned-111325-111225-006.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/39e874a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7178x4028+0+380/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F08%2Fef%2Fc441d8eb46cfb97cee0dfa649c79%2Fabughazaleharraigned-111325-111225-006.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Congressional candidate and social media influencer Kat Abughazaleh after her arraignment on a federal conspiracy charge at the Dirksen Federal Courthouse.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Arthur Maiorella/For the Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2026/tracker-federal-prosecutions-chicago-status-trumps-immigration-blitz-ice" >Tracking every known federal prosecution in Chicago tied to Trump’s deportation blitz</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>But defense attorneys say none of the damage<b> </b>was caused by the defendants themselves — a point they expect prosecutors<b> </b>to concede. Rather, the indictment generally accuses the four of bracing themselves with their hands and bodies against the vehicle,<b> </b>hindering its progress.</p><p>They did so after exercising their First Amendment rights “in a location specified for such protests by the Village of Broadview,” and after the agent decided to slowly drive<b> </b>into the crowd, according to the defense attorneys.</p><p>The agent did not wait for police to clear a path, did not identify himself and did not order the crowd to move aside, the attorneys argued.</p><p>“Their only actions — aside from bracing themselves against a vehicle that drove into a crowd of protesters — was being in that crowd of protesters,” the defense attorneys wrote. “Their actions are inseparable from the exercise of their constitutional right to assemble — in the exact location where Broadview police had allowed them to do so all morning."</p><p>Evan<b> </b>Bernick, an associate professor<b> </b>at Northern Illinois University<b> </b>College of Law, said he spoke with Straw<b> </b>and his attorneys<b> </b>about the motion filed Monday. He said the allegations in the case could have a chilling effect on protests beyond those against Midway Blitz. That's because of the concern that lawful protesters could be on the hook for the illegal actions of others through a conspiracy charge.</p><p>"This prosecution is not just about this prosecution," Bernick said. "It's about the prosecution of anybody who might go into public and engage in an assembly with peaceful purposes, with lawful purposes, and then decide not to on the belief that they might get targeted and successfully indicted, even though they have done nothing wrong."</p>
entries.author: Jon Seidel

entries.title: Mayor Brandon Johnson meets with men in prison working toward Northwestern University degrees
entries.summary: <p>For the past three weeks, Northwestern University student Anthony Ehlers has been painting a portrait of Mayor Brandon Johnson.</p><p>Ehlers has been working on the painting, which depicts the mayor signing an executive order concerning the National Guard, in anticipation of Johnson’s visit on Sunday to Sheridan Correctional Center, where Ehlers is incarcerated.</p><p>“He’s here on a Sunday when he usually is at church with his family; that’s pretty meaningful,” Ehlers said. “He knows we can’t vote, so he’s not stumping for votes; He’s really talking about issues that affect us.”</p><p>More than 100 people gathered in Sheridan’s gymnasium, about 70 miles southwest of Chicago. Students of the Northwestern Prison Education Program, which offers undergraduate degrees to men incarcerated at Sheridan, their families and professors had the opportunity to speak with the mayor.</p><p>In 2023, <a class="Link" href="https://news.wttw.com/2023/11/21/history-making-ceremony-men-prison-earn-degrees-northwestern-university-we-want-show-what" target="_blank" ><u>their initial graduating class</u></a> became the first group of incarcerated students to earn a bachelor’s degree from a top university. Northwestern also offers a bachelor’s degree program at the largest women’s prison in the state, Logan Correctional Center.</p><p>“I'm here to learn and ensure that my responsibility as a leader of the city in Chicago, that you all know that you will always be front and center in all of the decisions that I make,” Johnson said to the crowd.</p><p>In a fireside chat-style program, student Nikolas Gacho spoke about how he was expelled three times with little support from the five different schools he attended. Giovanni Rios told Johnson how every day he had had to split two loaves of bread with numerous family members for a meal. Hugo Ocon raised concerns over federal immigration raids across the city.</p><p>Following their personal statements, the students, now all graduates or graduates-to-be of Northwestern University, moved on to questions. They asked what Johnson’s administration is doing to strengthen public schools, enact compassionate immigration policy and support incarcerated people, as about 40% of people in Illinois prisons are sentenced in Cook County.</p><p>In his response, Johnson discussed his family’s own experience with the prison system, the necessity of educational investments, and the city’s Director of Reentry Joseph Mapp, who is tasked with providing Chicagoans leaving prison with access to housing, mental health support and job training.</p><p>“People who are closest to the danger, are closest to the solutions. We have to center our work around the people who have experienced these failed systems,” Johnson said.</p><p>For student Darvin Henderson, the mayor’s visit was “magical.”</p><p>“I didn’t come from a place where we meet people like that, like the mayor. … I didn’t even grow up around that,” Henderson said. “Those opportunities never even was available. And look, I’m in prison. People would think I’m at the bottom. And yet I’m meeting – rubbing elbows with people that’s at the top!”</p><p>As student Taki Peacock hopes to make his eventual release as easy as possible, he was interested to learn about the Office of Reentry.</p><p>“Leaving incarceration, where basically you’ve been taken care of your whole incarceration whether it be family members or the system itself taking care of you to having to go into society and provide for yourself completely, that’s a frightening perspective,” Peacock said.</p><p>Ian Valencia, a Northwestern senior incarcerated at Sheridan, has been locked up half his life, since he was 17.</p><p>The visit of a sitting Chicago mayor to a state prison – perhaps the first of its kind, according to Northwestern students – is symbolically important, Valencia said.</p><p>“It’s helping give me a more hopeful look on what’s possible, if more people get involved in trying to change what prison is supposed to be like,” he said.</p><p>Earlier in the week, students found out their family members could join the event.</p><p>It gave Rob Cunningham the opportunity to see her son Demetrius Cunningham play the keyboard for the first time. Cunningham bought Demetrius keyboard instructional books, who while in prison taught himself how to play using only<a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/written-inside/2017/03/21/ep-1-a-silent-piano" target="_blank" ><u> a piece of cardboard</u></a>. Cunningham said it was amazing to see Demetrius playing for the crowd.</p><p>Student Anthony Harris Jr.’s father drove eight hours from Memphis with less than a week’s notice to attend the event. It had been about a year and a half since father and son had seen each other. Anthony Harris Sr. said seeing the changes NPEP brings to students’ lives is a “blessing.”</p><p>William Shafiq Peeples, formerly on death row before Illinois abolished capital punishment, is serving a life sentence for murder. Peeples said he hopes the visit helped the mayor understand the value of educating people in prison – even those, like himself, who will never be released.</p><p>“So I understand when some people hear about the program and they say, ‘You’re giving all this education to a murderer.’ I think talking to him today will help him be able to explain to them why this is necessary,” Peeples said. “Education transforms human beings.”</p><p><i>This story is a collaboration between WBEZ and WTTW News.</i></p>
entries.author: Alex Keefe | WBEZ

entries.title: Freezing subminimum wage for tipped workers 'irresponsible,' Mayor Johnson says
entries.summary: <p>At a time when Chicago is becoming increasingly unaffordable for working-class residents, Mayor Brandon Johnson said Monday it would be “not only tone deaf, but irresponsible” for the City Council to stop phasing out the subminimum wage for tipped workers.</p><p>At Wednesday’s City Council meeting, Far Northwest Side Ald. <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2026/03/11/samantha-nugent-freeze-phaseout-chicago-subminimum-wage-tipped-workers" >Samantha Nugent (39th) </a>plans to use a parliamentary maneuver to resurrect a stalled proposal that would freeze the subminimum wage at 24% of the $16.60 minimum wage paid to hourly Chicago workers who do not receive tips.</p><p>Without the freeze, tipped workers now paid $12.62 an hour would receive a raise to 16% of Chicago’s minimum wage. That amount is reset every July 1. With the freeze, they would still receive a raise, but it would be capped at 24% of whatever the minimum hourly wage turns out to be.</p><p>In 2023, the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2023/10/6/23905473/minimum-wage-chicago-tipped-restaurant-workers-compromise-ordinance-passage-brandon-johnson" >City Council approved</a> a measure that phases out the subminimum wage for restaurant servers, bartenders and other tipped workers. That phaseout began in July 2024. </p><p>Nugent has argued that restaurant owners, particularly in wards like her own that border surrounding suburbs, can no longer afford the five-year phaseout at a time when rising property taxes, food and labor costs, on-again, off-again tariffs and increased license fees are eating into their razor-thin profits.</p><p>Johnson doesn’t buy that argument. Appearing Monday on WBEZ-FM’s monthly “Ask the Mayor” program, Johnson made it clear that he plans to fight to keep one of the signature wins of his progressive agenda.</p><p>“At a time in which … it’s becoming increasingly more difficult to afford to raise a family in this country — Democrats rolling back the wages of Black and Brown women — that is not only tone-deaf, but it’s irresponsible,” Johnson said. “Democrats have to pay attention to what the voters in the city of Chicago are calling for, and quite frankly, what voters across America are expecting from leadership."</p><p>Johnson said there is “uncertainty in the global economy” tied to the Iran war, the spike in oil prices that the war has triggered and, President Donald Trump’s on-again-off-again tariffs.</p><p>But he argued that the Chicago economy is doing fine, pointing to an 83% renewal rate for business licenses, a surge in summer travel to Chicago, and O’Hare Airport’s climb to the top of the charts as the nation’s busiest airport.</p><p>Far Southwest Side Ald. Matt O’Shea (19th) said he's hearing just opposite "every time I walk into" a bar or restaurant in his border ward.</p><p>"They are hanging on by a thread. They ‘re employing less people, and in some cases, they’ve closed the doors. Businesses are looking to move to the suburbs,” O’Shea said. “This is an effort to assist the hospitality industry that is struggling in neighborhoods all across our city."</p><p>O’Shea scoffed at Johnson’s claim that freezing the tipped wage would be “tone-deaf” to the affordability crisis sweeping the nation.</p><p>“It was tone-deaf when Black and Brown women who were working in the mayor’s office were being abused by leaders on the mayor’s team, and not a damned thing was done about it,” he said.</p><p>O'Shea was referring to Johnson’s former communications chief, Ronnie Reese, who was accused of sexual harassment and creating a hostile work environment months before being forced out of City Hall. Reese denied the allegations.</p><p>In order to override a mayoral veto, 24 alderpersons who voted to phase out the subminimum wage would need to change their votes. The vote at the time was 34-10.</p><p>North Side Ald. Bennett Lawson (44th), who proposed the freeze last year only to have it stall in committee, said there are “people flipping already” after getting an earful from the local restaurant owners.</p><p>“It’s a different coalition certainly than it was 2½ ago. Different time in the City Council. A lot of the folks who are calling for this to be heard were ‘yes’ votes,” Lawson said. “When we see a dramatic drop in the types of hours and the closure of restaurants, we need to take that seriously and work with our restaurant partners. It’s not just the big guys. It’s a lot of the little folks, the mom and pops in the neighborhoods, too.”</p><p></p><p>Illinois Restaurant Association President Sam Toia said the average server in an Illinois restaurant makes “close to $29 an hour,” and added that Washington, D.C., recently froze its tipped wage at 75% of the minimum wage. “If Mayor [Muriel] Bowser can realize it was a mistake, why can’t Mayor Johnson?” Toia asked.</p>
entries.author: Fran Spielman

entries.title: Review: Zara Larsson brings endless summer energy to Chicago on Midnight Sun Tour
entries.summary: <p>Y2k called and it wants nothing back from Zara Larsson. The queen of 2000s and 2010s core brought her Midnight Sun Tour to Riviera Sunday night, and as expected it was a midriff-bearing, pastel-drowning, glitter-bombing affair that proved time travel is possible with enough determination and cosplay.</p><p>For years, the Swedish pop star has been criticized for rehashing been there, done that tropes. As in, do we need another Britney Spears or can we just wait for the (hopeful) comeback? (Adding Spears’ “Gimme More” to her set list doesn’t help matters.) This was the overall feeling after seeing Larsson open for Tate McRae at the United Center last August. But it might’ve been a rush to judgment.</p><p>Given a larger stage and a full 90 minutes to fully display her powerhouse vocals and dance moves, Larsson proved she’s a strong contender in the current pop milieu that has largely been thriving off sequels, throwbacks and reinventions — all of which encompasses Larsson’s own story.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-390000" name="image-390000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Zara Larsson performs at the Riviera Theatre in the Uptown neighborhood of Chicago on Sunday.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Robin Bøe</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>First discovered as a 10-year-old child after she won the 2008 edition of Swedish talent show “Talang,” Larsson was quickly signed to a record deal and released a series of albums that imbued the spirit of her predecessors Robyn and Ace of Base, but largely only moved the needle in Europe. In 2026, something finally clicked.</p><p>Thanks to the TikTok algorithm bait and the explosion of a Lisa Frank dolphin meme that paired the upbeat track “Symphony” with the ‘90s designer’s colorful imagery, people are finally discovering Larsson's talent that has been lurking in the shadows all this time. Many have been clamoring to her 2025 Grammy-nominated album “Midnight Sun” (recently rereleased in a special edition designed by Lisa Frank herself) and the “Stateside” collab track with PinkPantheress, which recently got a bump after Olympic gold medalist Alysa Liu skated to the remix during an exhibition performance; it gave Larsson her first No. 1 position on the Spotify chart. But fans are also boosting songs Larsson wrote more than a decade ago — like the earworm 2015 club hit “Lush Life” — affording her the first Billboard hits of her career. The industry magazine has taken notice, too; in April, Billboard will recognize Larsson the Breakthrough Award at the annual Women in Music summit.</p><p>“I’ve been wanting to do this my whole life,” Larsson passionately shared to introduce her apropos song “Saturn’s Return” that featured some of her strongest vocals, edging in on Mariah Carey range. “You try to plan things or plan when it’s going to happen, and all you can do is follow along and try to be present and vulnerable,” she added. “I’m so happy to be here in this moment.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-d70000" name="image-d70000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f1612f5/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4760x2671+0+2233/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F25%2F47%2F93fbd6e34d34b49318ffddda7810%2Fzl-midnight-ustour-26-15-03-chicago-canon-robinboee-2234.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Austin takes a selfie with Zara Larsson after the fan was plucked from the crowd to dance with the Swedish pop star on “Lush Life” in a part of the set that has now gone viral.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Robin Bøe</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p><b></b> The feeling was mutual with her fans, especially one named Austin who was plucked from the crowd to dance with Larsson on “Lush Life” in a part of the set that has now gone viral; after nailing the moves, he received some of the biggest cheers of the night. Hundreds of other Gen Z’ers, kids and their parents flocked to the all-ages event in a bright display of rainbow sherbet ensembles and carrying inflatable sea creatures that have become the unofficial mascot of the tour. <b></b> In February, just weeks before the headlining performances kicked off, the entire trek completely sold out, and the demand felt palpable Sunday night. The show was massively packed with fans singing along to nearly every song and vying for front-row access; many were growingly frustrated that they were being denied entry into the first tier of the GA floor nearest the stage due to what security was calling “capacity” limits. In a better scenario, the show should’ve been upgraded to a larger room like the Aragon across the street.</p><p>But it was hard to stay mad too long with her sunshine-and-rainbows presence on stage. Larsson lives and breathes good vibes, an ethos she refers to as an “endless summer,” that comes across in visuals and in sound. As she whipped through club hits like “Midnight Sun” and the island pop track “Crush,” accompanied by her “Girl’s Girl” crew of women musicians, many in the crowd ditched the parkas they wore to get to Uptown. An ensemble of five fly girl dancers added to the vacation feel, showing off incredible synchronized choreography while wearing bikini tops, jean shorts and pool towels draped over their shoulders. It’ll be a good look a few months from now if the rumors are true that Larsson is playing Lollapalooza (we’ll find out Tuesday with the lineup drop). Whether she does or not, it will be a very #ZaraSummer with all eyes on the newest pop princess in town.</p><p><b></b></p><h3>Zara Larsson Set List for March 15, 2026 show at Riviera Theatre</h3><p>Midnight Sun<br>Blue Moon<br>Can’t Tame Her<br>I Would Like / Sundown<br>Love Me Land<br>All the Time<br>Pretty Ugly<br>Ain't My Fault<br>Hot & Sexy<br>Ammunition<br>Gimme More (Britney Spears cover)<br>Girl’s Girl<br>Wow<br>Ruin My Life<br>Saturn’s Return<br>The Ambition<br>Stateside (PinkPantheress collab)<br>Eurosummer<br>Crush<br>Lush Life<br>Never Forget You<br>Puss Puss<br>Midnight Sun<br>Symphony (Clean Bandit cover)</p>
entries.author: Selena Fragassi

entries.title: Sindicato de Maestros planea unirse al día de acción del 1 de mayo 'sin escuela, sin trabajo'
entries.summary: <p>El Sindicato de Maestros de Chicago (CTU) tiene la intención de llevar a cabo lo que equivale a una huelga de un día el 1 de mayo y está solicitando el apoyo oficial del alcalde y de la junta escolar para que los estudiantes puedan faltar a la escuela y participar.</p><p>El miércoles, la Casa de Delegados, compuesta por 730 miembros del sindicato, <a class="Link" href="https://www.ctulocal1.org/posts/mayday-resolution/?link_id=2&can_id=17ebc6e1a278c81ea80307c4c5703485&source=email-ctu-members-vote-to-join-may-1st-no-school-no-work-no-shopping-day-of-civic-action&email_referrer=email_3140500&email_subject=ctu-members-vote-to-join-may-1st-no-school-no-work-no-shopping-day-of-civic-action" target="_blank" >aprobó una resolución</a> para unirse a un movimiento nacional que hace un llamado a un día de "no escuela, no trabajo, no compras". Los participantes planean exigir mayores impuestos a los ricos, escuelas mejor financiadas, protecciones para inmigrantes y otras reformas.</p><p>Los líderes del sindicato citaron varias razones para participar, incluyendo “demandar que ICE salga de nuestras ciudades”, refiriéndose a la agencia del Servicio Inmigración y Control de Aduanas (ICE) y “cobrar impuestosa los ricos para apoyar nuestras escuelas y servicios vitales”, según la resolución.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-190000" name="module-190000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>También dice que la educación pública está enfrentando ataques de “políticos MAGA” que apoyan las políticas del presidente Donald Trump y “los intereses corporativos”.</p><p>Ese día, el sindicato quiere que los miembros y estudiantes participen en educación cívica “adecuada para su edad”, programación sobre historia laboral, registro de votantes y capacitación sobre conocimientos de derechos, así como en manifestaciones y marchas.</p><p>“Enseñar a nuestros estudiantes qué es la acción cívica requiere más que libros de texto cuando el Presidente envía agentes federales a ocupar nuestras ciudades y el Gobernador elige seguir otorgando beneficios fiscales a multimillonarios en lugar de ofrecer a nuestros estudiantes el día escolar que merecen”, dijo Jackson Potter, vicepresidente del CTU, en un comunicado.</p><p>El sindicato quiere que el alcalde Brandon Johnson y la junta escolar hagan del 1 de mayo “un día oficial de acción”, indicando que el alcalde puede <a class="Link" href="https://www.ilga.gov/Documents/legislation/ilcs/documents/010500050K26-1.htm" target="_blank" >apoyarse en una ley estatal</a> que permite a los estudiantes de secundaria y primaria una ausencia justificada por año para asistir a un “evento cívico”.</p><p>El CTU alentó a sus miembros a <a class="Link" href="https://actionnetwork.org/petitions/may-day-civic-action-day/?link_id=2&can_id=17ebc6e1a278c81ea80307c4c5703485&source=email-may-1st-no-work-no-school-no-shopping-teletownhall-monday-at-6-pm-for-more-2&email_referrer=email_3140628&email_subject=may-1st-no-work-no-school-no-shopping&&" target="_blank" >firmar una petición pidiendo al alcalde</a> que tome acción.</p><p>En un comunicado, Johnson dijo que “trabajaría con nuestros socios en CPS [Escuelas Públicas de Chicago] y en nuestras comunidades escolares para encontrar soluciones que aseguren que no haya pérdida de tiempo de instrucción”.</p><p>Sin embargo, indicó su apoyo al movimiento más amplio, diciendo que el esfuerzo por organizarse el 1 de mayo “ofrece a los trabajadores de todo el país la oportunidad de hacer escuchar su voz a través de la acción directa”.</p><p>CPS no respondió de inmediato a una solicitud de comentarios.</p><p>Kevin Moore, un miembro del CTU y maestro de estudios sociales en la Escuela Secundaria George Washington, dijo que es importante que el sindicato participe en la acción porque “la educación pública está bajo ataque” y “no deberíamos tolerar eso”.</p><p>Moore agregó que aunque los estudiantes pueden no estar en la escuela ese día, aún aprenderán algo.</p><p>“Estamos enseñando a nuestros estudiantes cómo usar su voz”, dijo Moore. “Estamos enseñando a nuestros estudiantes que tienen un papel muy poderoso en la defensa de la democracia y que tienen un papel en la construcción del país que necesitamos, en el futuro que merecen”.</p><p>CTU ha participado antes en acciones similares de un día. <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/chicago/2016/04/01/chicago-teachers-stage-rare-1-day-strike" target="_blank" >En 2016, los maestros llevaron a cabo una rara huelga de un día</a> en apoyo a salarios más altos y más financiamiento estatal. Esa acción llevó a CPS a cancelar las clases por el día y establecer sitios de contingencia donde los padres que necesitaban trabajar podían dejar a sus hijos. CPS presentó un cargo ante la Junta de Relaciones Laborales Educativas de Illinois alegando que la huelga era ilegal.</p><p>Realizar acciones en el Día Internacional de los Trabajadores, también conocido como Día del Trabajo, tiene una larga historia. Los orígenes de esta festividad se remontan al Haymarket Affair de Chicago de 1886, <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/reset-with-sasha-ann-simons/2023/05/01/may-day-marks-chicagos-history-of-fighting-for-workers-rights" target="_blank" >una huelga por los derechos de los trabajadores</a> donde murieron varios manifestantes, policías y civiles.</p><p>A mediados de la década de 2000, cientos de miles de personas participaron en marchas del Día del Trabajo para apoyar los derechos de los inmigrantes. El 1 de mayo del año pasado, el CTU también participó en manifestaciones y marchas. Aunque fue un día escolar regular para los estudiantes, el sindicato informó que alrededor de <a class="Link" href="https://www.ctulocal1.org/posts/may-day-2025-history-made-lets-keep-it-going/" target="_blank" >500 estudiantes participaron en la marcha.</a></p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por</i> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
entries.author: Emmanuel Camarillo

entries.title: Ex-Bears CB Nahshon Wright leads NFL with $1.4 million in performance-based bonus pay
entries.summary: <p>Former <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears" target="_blank" >Bears</a> cornerback <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/02/03/bears-cb-nahshon-wright-embraces-uncertainty-as-he-heads-toward-likely-windfall-in-free-agency" >Nahshon Wright</a> earned an NFL-high $1.4 million in performance-based bonus pay when the league distributed it Monday. That more than doubled his pay after playing on a one-year, $1.1 million contract last season.</p><p>Wright, 27, bounced around the league before cashing in on his opportunity with the Bears last season. He had a career-high five interceptions, including one he returned for a touchdown, and was a Pro Bowl alternate.</p><p>He left in free agency last week on a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/03/10/bears-jets-nahshon-wright-agrees-free-agent-deal-interceptions-fumble-receovery-forced-nfl" >one-year, $3.5 million contract with the Jets</a>.</p><p>The NFL gave players $542 million in performance-based bonuses to young players using a formula based predominantly on snap counts. Browns safety Ronnie Hickman received the next-highest bonus at $1.3 million, followed closely by Falcons offensive tackle Elijah Wilkinson and Panthers safety Nick Scott.</p><p>The Bears will move forward at cornerback primarily with Jaylon Johnson on the outside and Kyler Gordon playing nickel. The other outside spot is up for competition among several players, and perhaps some incoming rookies, but mainly Tyrique Stevenson and Terell Smith.</p><p></p><bsp-list-loadmore class="PageListH" entries.title: Federal judge extends order blocking Trump cuts to Illinois public health funding
entries.summary: <p>A federal judge in Chicago has extended an order blocking the Trump administration from cutting $600 million in public heath grants to Illinois and three other Democratic-led states.</p><p>U.S. District Judge Manish S. Shah issued a preliminary injunction Friday in the Northern District of Illinois Court, extending <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/health/2026/02/12/federal-judge-temporarily-blocks-trump-cuts-to-hiv-lead-poisoning-prevention-funds" target="_blank" >a temporary restraining order he issued last month</a> that was set to expire.</p><p>The four targeted states — Illinois, Minnesota, Colorado and California — <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/health/2026/02/11/federal-cuts-hiv-lead-poisoning-prevention-grants-raoul" target="_blank" >filed a federal lawsuit </a>in February.</p><p>The funding cuts would have targeted HIV tracking, lead poisoning prevention, family planning and other public health initiatives across the four states. In Illinois, the planned cuts targeted at least $29 million in grants funding the American Medical Association in Illinois, which supports gender-affirming care, and various HIV prevention programs.</p><p>In the injunction, Shah said the cuts could have led to “irreparable harm” and that the public interest favored preserving the funding. Any actions taken to halt payments to the targeted states from the Department of Health and Human Services and the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention should be considered “null, void, and rescinded,” Shah wrote.</p><p>The judge set another hearing in the case for next Monday.</p><p>Illinois Attorney General Kwame Raoul previously said the cuts would lead to the loss of 100 jobs at the Illinois Department of Public Health and the end of lead poisoning prevention grants in 25 local health departments.</p><p>“Targeting four Democrat-run states that are standing up to his completely unrelated immigration policies is a transparent attempt to bully us into compliance,” Raoul said in a statement after Shah issued the temporary restraining order. “We remain unflinching in our commitment to defending against the Trump administration’s continued unlawful directives intended to force us to implement immigration and other unrelated policies.”</p><p>Raoul did not immediately respond to requests for comment about the preliminary injunction.</p><p>The U.S. Department of Health and Human Services didn’t immediately respond to requests for comment.</p>
entries.author: Mary Norkol

entries.title: Bears transaction tracker: Who's coming, going this offseason
entries.summary: <p>The <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears" target="_blank" >Bears</a> have been busy with a flurry of moves as they try to sharpen their roster — and make prudent salary cap decisions. </p><p>They can start agreeing to deals with free agents Monday, though players can't officially sign their contracts until the start of the league year Wednesday.</p><p>Here's a look at who the Bears have gained and lost: </p><h3>March 16</h3><p>Former Bears defensive tackle <b>Andrew Billings</b> has agreed to a one-year deal with the Cardinals. Billings spent the last three years with the Bears but was unlikely to return, especailly after the team signed two defensive tackles in the first few days of free agency last week.</p><h3><b>March 14</b></h3><h3></h3><p>Former Bears returner <b>Devin Duvernay</b> is heading to the Cardinals on a one-year deal, per Jordan Schultz of Schultz Report. Duvernay's departure seemed likely after the Bears signed Kalif Raymond last week.</p><p></p><h3><b>March 13</b></h3><p>Cornerback and special teamer <b>Jaylon Jones</b> is returning on a one-year deal, sources confirmed to the Sun-Times.</p><p>Safety <b>Jonathan Owens</b> agreed to sign <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/03/13/bears-jonathan-owens-simone-biles-colts-lose-safety-free-agent-one-year" target="_blank" >a one-year contract to join the Colts</a>, a source confirmed Friday. Owens, a backup and special teamer, is married to gymnast Simone Biles.</p><p></p><h3>March 12</h3><p>Former Bears safety <b>Jaquan Brisker</b> agreed to a one-year deal to <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/03/12/bears-steelers-safety-jaquan-brisker-heading-home-pittsburgh-nfl-free-agency" target="_blank" >play for his hometown Steelers.</a></p><p>Safety-turned-cornerback <b>C.J. Gardner-Johnson</b>, who played well over 10 games after joining the Bears midseason, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/03/12/bears-steelers-safety-jaquan-brisker-heading-home-pittsburgh-nfl-free-agency" target="_blank" >is signing with the Bills</a>.</p><p>Former Bears tight end <b>Durham Smythe</b> signed a one-year deal to rejoin former offensive coordinator Declan Doyle in Baltimore.</p><p>Guard <b>Jordan McFadden</b>, who started in the Bears' playoff loss to the Rams, is returning to the team on a one-year deal. McFadden played 13 games and started twice for the Chargers from 2023-24.</p><h3>March 11</h3><p>Former Browns left tackle <b>Jedrick Wills</b> will join the Bears' starting competition. He'll sign a one-year deal, a source confirmed to the Sun-Times. Wills is a former No. 10 overall pick who started 45 games for the Browns from 2020-22 before being limited by injury the next two years. He sat out last year because of a knee injury. </p><p>Former Bears safety <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/03/11/bears-star-safety-kevin-byard-to-sign-with-patriots-report" target="_blank" >Kevin Byard</a> is signing a one-year deal with the Patriots to reunite with former Titans coach Mike Vrabel. The Bears said this offseason they'd hope to work out a deal with their team captain and Pro Bowl player but seemed to move on when they gave Coby Bryant a three-year, $40 million deal Monday.</p><p>The Bears agreed to re-sign backup safety <b>Elijah Hicks</b> on a one-year contract Wednesday, a source said. He was a seventh-round pick in general manager Ryan Poles' first draft class in 2022 and has played 61 games (15 starts) for the Bears.</p><p>Former Bears defensive lineman <b>Chris Williams</b> jumped to the Falcons, who now are run by general manager Ian Cunningham, on a one-year, $2.4 million deal, ESPN reported.</p><h3>March 10</h3><p>The Bears added to their defensive line depth Tuesday when they agreed to sign <b>Kentavius Street</b> to a one-year deal, a source confirmed to the Sun-Times. He's played for four teams in his seven years in the league.</p><p>Street spent 2022 playing for the Saints, where Bears defensive coordinator Dennis Allen served as head coach. He posted a career-high 3 ½ sacks that season and has only 10 ½ in his career. Pro Football Focus rated him as the 122<sup>nd</sup>-best run defender among the 224 eligible interior defensive linemen in their system last year. He spent a career-high 104 snaps over tackle in 2022, his year playing for Allen.</p><p>The Bears are signing former Bills defensive back <b>Cam Lewis</b> to a two-year deal, a source confirmed to the Sun-Times on Tuesday.</p><p>Like Coby Bryant, whom the Bears signed a day earlier, Lewis has experience at cornerback, safety and in the slot.</p><p>Lewis, who turns 29 next month, went to college at Buffalo and then spent the first six years of his career with the Bills. He started only 14 games, but 10 of those came over the past two years. Lewis played 38% of the Bills defensive snaps but was a special teams stalwart, playing on 51% of their kicking downs. He’s played at least 51% of his team’s special teams snaps in each of the past four years.</p><p>Former Bears cornerback <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/03/10/bears-jets-nahshon-wright-agrees-free-agent-deal-interceptions-fumble-receovery-forced-nfl" ><b>Nahshon Wright </b></a>agreed to a one-year, $5.5 million deal with the Jets, per NFL Network. Wright had five interceptions and started all but one game for the Bears last season, one year after the Vikings kept him on their practice squad for most of the season.</p><p> <br>The Bears agreed to a one-year, $5.1 million deal with former Lions wide receiver and return man <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/03/10/bears-to-sign-former-lions-wide-receiver-returner-kalif-raymond-source" ><b>Kalif Raymond</b></a>. He had 24 catches for 289 yards and a touchdown for the Lions last season.</p><h3>March 9</h3><p>Third-string quarterback <b>Case Keenum</b>, who was a key part of Caleb Williams’ development last year under coach Ben Johnson, is coming back to the Bears, a source confirmed. He’ll make as $5.5 million and as much as $8 million over two years, per ESPN.</p><p>Keenum hasn't thrown a pass since 2023 but has been part of the growth of both Williams and Texans quarterback C.J. Stroud late in his career.</p><p>“He has been an integral part of what we’ve done,” Johnson said in January, days after the Bears’ emotional playoff win against the Packers. “He has been tremendous in terms of Caleb’s growth. That was really the thought process of bringing him in, is: the guy with skins on the wall, he has played in big games himself on a high level.</p><p>"He has won big games, he has lost big games ... it’s something that all these young players can really lean into, particularly in weeks like this where you come off an emotional win and yet you got to turn your sights on the next page pretty quickly."</p><p>After one of the best seasons of his career, former <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears" target="_blank" >Bears</a> linebacker <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/03/09/former-bears-linebacker-tremaine-edmunds-gets-3-year-36-million-deal-from-giants-report" ><b>Tremaine Edmunds</b></a> got a big contract offer from the Giants. He agreed Monday to a three-year, $36 million deal, sources confirmed to the Sun-Times. It includes $24 million guaranteed.</p><p>Former Bears defensive end <b>Dominique Robinson</b>, a fifth-round pick in 2022, is signing with the Texans on a one-year, $4 milliion deal, ESPN reported.</p><p>The Bears will sign linebacker <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/03/09/bears-to-sign-playmaking-veteran-devin-bush-jr-as-they-rebuild-linebacker-corps-source" ><b>Devin Bush Jr.</b> </a>from the Browns to a three-year, $30 million deal after the departure of Edmunds, a source said. Bush, a former No. 10 overall pick, had three interceptions and two forced fumbles last season.</p><p>Former Colts defensive tackle <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/03/09/bears-first-move-in-free-agency-is-deal-with-dt-neville-gallimore-source" ><b>Neville Gallimore</b></a> is joining the Bears on a two-year, $12 million deal when the new league year opens, a source said. He played all 17 games in Indianapolis last season, including eight starts.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">Bears Nation let’s do it!!!! Thank you for the opportunity. Let’s work! <a href="https://twitter.com/hashtag/BearDown?src=hash&ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">#BearDown</a> 🐻</p>— Neville Gallimore (@Path2Greatwork) <a href="https://twitter.com/Path2Greatwork/status/2031059927179972657?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">March 9, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>Linebacker <b>D'Marco Jackson</b> is returning to the Bears on a two-year, $7.5 million deal, a source confirmed. The Bears claimed Jackson on cut day during training camp, citing his experience in coordinator Dennis Allen's system while in New Orleans. He proved to be a solid special teams player and a worthy spot starter. In Week 15, he was named NFC Defensive Player of the Week after logging the first sack and interception of his career in a win against the Browns. </p><p>Receiver <b>Olamide Zaccheaus</b> is leaving the Bears for Atlanta, a source confirmed, agreeing to sign a contract with the Falcons. Zaccheaus spent one year in Chicago after helping the Commanders develop quarterback Jayden Daniels. In Chicago, he struggled with drops — his six were tied for 13<sup>th</sup>-most in the NFL — and by the end of the season was behind DJ Moore, Rome Odunze and Luther Burden on the Bears’ depth chart. He finished with 30 catches for 315 yards. In Atlanta, he’ll be reunited with new general manager Ian Cunningham, who was the Bears’ assistant GM last season. Zaccheaus played in Atlanta from 2019-22, catching passes from new president of football Matt Ryan, the former quarterback.</p><p>With none of their top four safeties under contract for this upcoming season, the Bears needed to move quickly to grab capable players at the position. They found that and more when they <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/03/09/bears-sign-seahawks-coby-bryant-safety-nfl" target="_blank" >agreed to sign</a> the Seahawks' <b>Coby Bryant</b>. He'll get a three-year, $40 million deal, per ESPN. Bryant, who had four interceptions last year, can play both free safety and strong safety. It's unclear if his additions means Kevin Byard's Bears tenure is over. Both sides had been optimistic about getting a deal done for Byard, who mostly played free safety.</p><h3><b>March 8</b></h3><p>Defensive end <b>Daniel Hardy</b>, who has been a special teams ace, agreed to sign a two-year deal worth as much as $6 million to return to the Bears, a source confirmed to the Sun-Times on Sunday. Hardy played only 5% of the Bears' defensive snaps last year but appeared in a whopping 78% of their kicking plays, totaling 22 tackles.</p><h3><b>March 6</b></h3><p>The day <b>Drew Dalman</b> put in his retirement paperwork, the Bears traded a 2027 fifth-round draft pick to the Patriots for his replacement, center <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/03/06/bears-trade-garrett-bradbury-patriots-center-2027-fifth-round-pick-nfl" target="_blank" ><b>Garrett Bradbury</b></a>. Bradbury, who started every game for the AFC champions last year, has one year left on a $9.6 million contract he signed last year after spending six years with the Vikings.</p><h3><b>March 5</b></h3><p>Needing to clear cap space and cognizant of their depth at wide receiver, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/halas-intrigue/2026/03/05/halas-intrigue-podcast-breaking-down-bears-dj-moore-trade-nfl" target="_blank" >the Bears traded</a> <b>DJ Moore</b> and a fifth-round pick to the Bills for a second-round draft pick this year. Moore jad just 682 receiving yards on 50 catches last year, the worst numbers of his career. Had the Bears kept Moore, he would have cost a $24.5 million salary cap hit, the third-highest on the team.</p><p>Eight days after telling linebacker <b>Tremaine Edmunds</b> and his agent that they could seek a trade, the Bears told Edmunds <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/03/05/bears-release-tremaine-edmunds-linebacker-salary-cap-nfl" target="_blank" >they were cutting him</a>. They saved $15 million in cap space in the process. Edmunds had four interceptions in 13 games last year, the second-most among all NFL linebackers.</p><h3><b>March 3</b></h3><p>Center <b>Drew Dalman</b>, who played every snap and reached the Pro Bowl in his first year with the Bears, told the team he planned to retire after just five years in the NFL. He filed his retirement paperwork three days later.</p><p></p><p></p><p></p><p></p><p></p><bsp-list-loadmore class="PageListH" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" data-hide-dates="true" data-show-loadmore="true" ><a class="AnchorLink" id="list-bc0001" name="list-bc0001"></a><div class="PageList-header" > <div class="PageList-header-title-wrap" > <div class="PageList-header-title"> <a class="" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears" target="{borderColor=#dfdfdf, pageNumber=1, inverseColors=false, footerBgColor=#000000, bodyFont=font-3, showListNumbers=false, imagePositionHorizontal=null, rteBodyFont=font-3, headerBottomBorderColor=#111010, titleUrl=https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears, subTitle=null, items=[{type=Image, title=Ex-Bears CB Nahshon Wright leads NFL with $1.4 million in performance-based bonus pay, url=https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/03/16/ex-bears-cb-nahshon-wright-leads-league-with-1-4-million-in-nfls-performance-based-bonus-pay, _styledTemplate=null, description=[{items=[The money from the bonus pool more than doubled Wright’s pay from last season. entries.title: Hit a breaking point? Chicago Heights 'rage room' lets visitors smash plates, printers for a fee
entries.summary: <p>Breaking Point Rage Room’s first client was a young mother, who arrived crying with her baby.</p><p>She left her infant with Breaking Point owner Melody Billups and headed into one of its rage rooms, where a range of dishes, appliances and glassware was set up for her to break with a baseball bat.</p><p>"She was crying, and she was just like, 'I needed this because I felt like I was gonna hurt myself or my baby,’” Billups said. “And that solidified that we were supposed to open."</p><p>That was in 2021, when Breaking Point opened a 600-square-foot space in Homewood. After closing the location in 2023, Billups reopened the all-ages space last month in Chicago Heights.</p><p>Rage rooms, or rooms that visitors can rent to destroy often easily-breakable items, with equipment like baseball bats and hammers, have been popular for more than a decade. The idea is to release stress, anger or other difficult emotions by breaking everything in sight.</p><p>Billups, 49, first heard of the concept several years ago when watching an episode of the FOX TV series “9-1-1.” After seeing the characters at a rage room, she decided to try it out herself.</p><p>"I went into my garage one day after a little spat with my significant other, and I went in there and just broke a couple of vases. And I felt so much better,” Billups said. “I came out of that garage, and I told my kids, 'We're opening a rage room.'"</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/db3d115/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+192/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F01%2F81%2F9ebce048434cb5576257cbb33366%2Frageroom-03xx26-04.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/6787ed9/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+192/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F01%2F81%2F9ebce048434cb5576257cbb33366%2Frageroom-03xx26-04.jpg data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Breaking Point Rage Room Owner Melody Billups</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Zubaer Khan/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Despite its global popularity, rage room businesses aren’t common in the Chicago area, with only one, Angry Cactus Lounge in Bucktown, located in Chicago. There are similar concepts, mostly offering activities like axe throwing, such as Naperville’s Axe Zone Lounge and Bulls Eye Axe Lounge in Burr Ridge and Glenview.</p><p>Breaking Point, about 30 miles south of Chicago at 191 W. Joe Orr Road, appears to be the only location that strictly offers rage rooms. Its new 1,800-square-foot space has three rooms.</p><p>Visitors are provided with safety gear and can break about 20 items such as wine or beer bottles. Billups also encourages visitors to bring their own items. Additional objects, like printers or monitors, are available as an add-on purchase.</p><p>Prices are $50 per person for 15 minutes, or $75 per person for 30 minutes. Groups are allowed, but solo raging is common. Screaming is also encouraged.</p><p>Billups estimates the business will be able to triple its revenue, compared to its old, smaller Homewood location. The business is also run by her nephew, managing partner Devin Stewart, and her four sons.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-6d0000" name="image-6d0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ede5fa5/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+397/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fbc%2F4c%2Fdb89191e4bffab55359d478b413e%2Frageroom-03xx26-10.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/667e1fe/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+397/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fbc%2F4c%2Fdb89191e4bffab55359d478b413e%2Frageroom-03xx26-10.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ba7cd54/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+397/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fbc%2F4c%2Fdb89191e4bffab55359d478b413e%2Frageroom-03xx26-10.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Three rage rooms are separated by plastic dividers at Breaking Point Rage Room." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ba7cd54/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+397/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fbc%2F4c%2Fdb89191e4bffab55359d478b413e%2Frageroom-03xx26-10.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e6fe72c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+397/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fbc%2F4c%2Fdb89191e4bffab55359d478b413e%2Frageroom-03xx26-10.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ba7cd54/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+397/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fbc%2F4c%2Fdb89191e4bffab55359d478b413e%2Frageroom-03xx26-10.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Three rage rooms are separated by plastic dividers at Breaking Point Rage Room.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Zubaer Khan/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Unlike most rage room businesses, mental health is at the forefront. Billups is looking to partner with therapists, who can bring their clients. She said support groups have also frequented the space.</p><p>“It's not just a regular place to go. I hug people when they come out,” Billups said. “Sometimes they just want to sit and talk about what brought them in here. … I may not be able to solve your problems. I can't do anything about your issues, but I can give you a shoulder to cry on, and I can give you a safe space.”</p><p>This means the business has sometimes brought some unexpected heaviness into their lives. </p><p>A young man, whose father died, recently stopped into Breaking Point. His aunt was scared for him and booked a room when he was struggling with his mental health.</p><p>“I felt like I was putting myself in his footsteps, in his shoes,” Stewart, 33, said. “I was feeling what he was going through. It was definitely heavy, but it was more so for me. I felt like I was a relief for him because I was assisting him in getting that anger out. We tried to make the vibe for him as safe and comfortable as we can. And I feel like that it was like a duty for me to just provide whatever I can to help him get that out.”</p><p>Opening the Black-owned business in Chicago Heights was intentional, Billups said. She felt the area was “underserved.”</p><p>“Our first client, when she came in and looked at me and she said, ‘I trust you.’ I said, 'That‘s something we need in Black and brown communities to feel safe,'” she said. “I don't think she would have been so comfortable if she didn't see my face when she came in.”</p><p>Sometimes, Billups and the team find themselves letting visitors extend their booking, like in the case of the young man who initially booked a 15 minute session.</p><p>“Forty-five minutes later, he's still in there, and we're passing him printers because he just needed it,” Billups said. “When he came out, he just kept thanking us and thanking us.”</p><p>It’s not uncommon for customers to ask for Billups’ personal phone number after stopping by.</p><p>“To not have an outlet or not have a listening ear, it could be the difference between life and death,” she said. “I can give you a space where you come in and feel like for those 15, 30 minutes that you are unleashing this heaviness off your back. It's fun, but it’s also cathartic.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-3a0000"
